Well, this should be fixed in `pook@v0.2.1`, but I think there's an implicit limitation in the `mocket` engine implementation that does not allow registering it at global level, which is very handy.

This code works:
```js
import pook
import requests
from mocket.plugins.pook_mock_engine import MocketEngine

# NOTE: this will not work and should be revised
# pook.set_mock_engine(MocketEngine)


@pook.on
def test_pook_engine():
    # Use Mocket as mock engine
    pook.set_mock_engine(MocketEngine)

    url = 'http://twitter.com/api/1/foobar'
    status = 200
    response_json = {'error': 'foo'}

    mock = pook.get(
        url,
        persist=True,
        reply=status,
        response_json=response_json,
    )

    requests.get(url)
    assert mock.calls == 1

    resp = requests.get(url)
    assert resp.status_code == status
    assert resp.json() == response_json
    assert mock.calls == 2


if __name__ == '__main__':
    test_pook_engine()
```

Yes, I searched for the standard info too and found the same thing, void pointers are pointers to object and casting to function pointer is implementation defined. (however Posix does define the cast, but that is still not in the ISO standard)

I'm not sure that I agree with your conclusion, the implementation defined behavior is still there even if one would turn off the warnings in the compiler, so I would not call that a solution.

My thinking is that moving the necessary cast (at least working with SDL) outside glad would at least keep glad compliant to the standard and leave the decision about lowering warning levels vs tricking the compiler to be silent up to the user.

Best Regards /Anders

    dec_val -= (dec_val/mask)*mask
    mask /= 256
print '.'.join(str(x) for x in ip)
```

> Yes, I'm wondering about a scenario where a user aliases a fish function, I should probably check that the aliased command is also not in the dict of aliases.

In this scenario, the `overridden` set [should already contain](https://github.com/nvbn/thefuck/wiki/Shell-aliases#fish) the _aliased_ function. :smiley:

> However on the command line the default alias name fuck is intended to be used as a verb, which I do not find appropriate.
> I would prefer if the default, documented alias name would be the magic word please.

Default alias will stay `fuck`, just use another alias:
```bash
eval "$(thefuck --alias please)"
```

> Is there a better way of doing this or are there any ideas on how cget could support this in the future?

I have not tested it but this should work(run in the source directory):

```
cget build --target install
```

Also, you can specify the install directory with `CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX` if you don't like the default:

```
cget build -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/usr/local --target install
```

> Also, is setting CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH to the cget prefix path the recommended way of using cget with IDEs like CLion?

That is one way, although I am not very familiar with CLion. Cget provides a toolchain file in `$CGET_PREFIX/cget/cget.cmake` that sets everything needed in cmake. So if you want to run cmake manually you can do:

```
mkdir build
cd build
cmake -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=$CGET_PREFIX/cget/cget.cmake ..
```

I don't know if there is a way in CLion to specify a toolchain file or not, but it is a better choice.

Just a few thoughts:

1. It is standard in the matplotlib API to see plotting functions that take kwargs of different types to set attributes of the plot. See the the `c` argument in [`plt.scatter`](http://matplotlib.org/api/pyplot_api.html#matplotlib.pyplot.scatter) for an example of this and the [source](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/master/lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py#L3815) for how it's handled. Under the hood (in `draw_nodes` in `circos.py`) we manually add patches to the axes object but I was thinking perhaps we could use `plt.scatter` instead to leverage this functionality.
2. I think having `set_nodecolors` and `set_edgecolors` behave in this fashion (with their corresponding kwargs) would keep things streamlined with the matplotlib API. This would also keep a low barrier to customization if you already know matplotlib. If we can offload handling these parameters to existing matplotlib functions as well, that would be advantageous.
3. The `set_*` methods are currently called internally when the plot is created. I'm not sure of a use case where they would need to be called after the plot is already created a-la `BasePlot.set_*()`
4. Since matplotlib is our "backend" so-to-speak, any valid [matplotlib color](http://matplotlib.org/api/colors_api.html) would be valid in nxviz.

Thanks @alanfischer. Am I right in thinking that you want to poll the `VeraSceneController` every 5 seconds from HA? If so there is an easier way to do this that doesn't require you to change the subscription code.

HA is set up to poll devices on a regular basis - which vera devices turn off because they update via subscription. However I think the only notification for scenes would be for the `active` property - so subscription basically won't work for the attributes you want.

So if in your home assistant device you override`should_poll` and return `true` - then HA will poll your device without needing any of the subscription changes (you can see the base vera `should_poll` here https://github.com/home-assistant/home-assistant/blob/dev/homeassistant/components/vera.py#L154). Your update method will then get polled.

The default polling interval in HA is set by the base class of each device (10s for a switch) - but it can be overridden (I think the insteon switch does this).

So I think you could simplify the changes - and still get the result you want.

This is almost certainly happening because you have multiple versions of libpng installed on your machine.  The code is compiled against one version of libpng, then run with another which then gives you an error because the versions don't match.  The right solution is definitely not to hack the version string to match.  The whole point of that version number is to cause an error in this specific case.  If you just hack it in it's very likely something bad will happen, like random program crashes.  

I just updated the dlib error message to be more informative when this happens.  It will now print the two versions of libpng in play here and tell you this is happening.

The right think to do is to make sure you only link to one version of libpng and that it's the version you are compiling with your code.

```
dt (np):  2.4073729515075684

dt (fmt_unroll200):  2.2189409732818604
```

where

```python
def unrollx(ff, x):
    return "\n".join("f " + " ".join(ff.format('{' + str(i+(j*3)) + '}') 
                                     for i in range(3)) 
                     for j in range(x))
    
def format_unroll(faces, face_type = 'v', unroll = 5):
    face_formats = {'v': '{0}', 'vt': '{0}/{0}', 'vn': '{0}//{0}', 'vtn': '{0}/{0}/{0}'}
    rowf = unrollx(face_formats[face_type], unroll)
    out_str = "\n".join(rowf.format(*row) for row in faces.reshape((-1,unroll*3)) + 1)
    return out_str
```

indeed, reading enum fields were much slower than reading normal ints, but most of the slowdown was caused (only on CPython) by the fact that Enum classes were generated dynamically, and thus Cython could not do a static lookup.
The commits from 45c5c6cd to e13a61b5 refactored how they are handled (and they use prebuilt instances as well). Now reading an enum field is only ~10% slower than a normal integer.

Escaping rules should not vary based on the number of arguments - consider something like `db.query("select * from T where name like '%%foo%%' and parent_id in (" + ",".join(["%s"] * len(ids)) + ")"), ids)`. The `like` clause shouldn't be escaped differently based on how the second part of the query is constructed.  In the low-level DB-API they can distinguish between an argument of None and an empty list, but we can't because we use `*args`, so our only option is to treat every query as if it is parameterized and unconditionally escape percent signs.

the script needs to keep running and updating in order to get jobs executed... 

for example:

    schedule.every(2).minutes.do(job)

this will schedule a job to execute every 2 minutes, but to get jobs executed you need to keep updating the schedule:

    while True:
        schedule.run_pending()
        time.sleep(1)

the run_pending function is the one that actually executes the scheduled jobs.

Placebo is definitely valuable if you have an account and want to make run deterministic, repeatable tests without touching AWS (at least most of the time).  For example, in a project I'm working right now, I have a test suite which will spin up fresh EC2 instances and run tests on them, and then when the test is done it will terminate the instances.  Placebo records the EC2 interactions on the first go around (record mode), and then any time after that, when the tests are run, the recorded EC2 interactions are played back instead of using AWS (playback mode).

Doing it this way allows me to record the tests with valid credentials, redact the credentials (substituting with fake ones), and then check in all of my source code including the cassettes.  Since everything is now redacted, all of my tests will pass (tox and Travis CI) without valid credentials _even_ when I don't have an internet connection. The thing is, I need to have the recording first.

Here's some sample code from my recent project which allows me to rerecord and playback quite easily (I'm using pytest and pytest-variables):
```python
def get_aws_info(input_dict):
    return (input_dict.get('AWS_ACCESS_KEY', 'fake_access_key'),
            input_dict.get('AWS_SECRET_KEY', 'fake_secret_key'),
            input_dict.get('AWS_REGION_NAME', 'us-west-2'))


@pytest.fixture()
def boto3_session_mock(variables):
    aws = get_aws_info(variables)
    session = boto3.Session(aws_access_key_id=aws[0],
                            aws_secret_access_key=aws[1],
                            region_name=aws[2])

    pill = placebo.attach(session, data_path='tests/fixtures/aws_recordings')

    pill.record() if variables.get('RECORD_BOTO', False) else pill.playback()

    return session
```

I also have a tox environment to help me rerecord easily:
```
[testenv:rerecord]
deps = -r{toxinidir}/test-requirements.txt
whitelist_externals = /bin/bash
commands =
    /bin/bash -c 'rm -f {toxinidir}/tests/fixtures/aws_recordings/*.json'
    coverage run -m pytest {posargs} --variables placebo_record.json tests/test_awsrun_connection.py
```

If it helps, [vcrpy](https://github.com/kevin1024/vcrpy) is another popular record/playback library used for HTTP requests.  I used that library before placebo, and it definitely helped me understand the concepts.

Problem command is: `aaa authentication fail-message ^`  [documentation](http://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/ios-xml/ios/sec_usr_aaa/configuration/15-sy/sec-usr-aaa-15-sy-book/sec-message-banners.html)

proper command (in my yaml file)
```yaml
    - |
        aaa authentication fail-message ^
        +-------------------------------------------------------------+
        |      All Connection attempts are recorded.                  |
        |-------------------------------------------------------------|
        |  If you are authorized and having trouble connecting please |
        |  contact (###)###-####.                                     |
        +-------------------------------------------------------------+
        ^
```
In this formar pyyaml will parse the command, and netmiko will be able to set it properly.

@craffel: I ran into a small hitch and want to run it by you.
As far as I've seen, multi-f0 algorithms report scores as a time stamp followed by a variable number of f0 values:
```
29.52	246.94	622.25	311.13	246.94
29.53	246.94	622.25	311.13	246.94
29.54	622.25	311.13	246.94
29.55	622.25	311.13	246.94
29.56	622.25	311.13	246.94	185.00	440.00
29.57	622.25	311.13	246.94	185.00	440.00	92.50
29.58	622.25	311.13	246.94	185.00	440.00	92.50
29.59	622.25	311.13	246.94	440.00	92.50
29.60	622.25	311.13	246.94	440.00	92.50	185.00
29.61	622.25	311.13	246.94	440.00	92.50	185.00	220.00
```
```mir_eval/melody.py``` is [resampling](https://github.com/craffel/mir_eval/blob/master/mir_eval/melody.py#L190) the estimate time series to ensure the time stamps are consistent with the reference time series. MIREX avoids the resampling problem entirely by requiring a particular time base.
This is all fine until you notice that the above excerpt has the following form (letters correspond to f0's belonging to a contiguous contour):
```
29.50	A	B	C
29.51	A	B	C
29.52	A	B	C	D
29.53	A	B	C	D
29.54	B	C	D
29.55	B	C	D
29.56	B	C	D	E	F
29.57	B	C	D	E	F	G
29.58	B	C	D	E	F	G	
29.59	B	C	D	F	G	
29.60	B	C	D	F	G	E
29.61	B	C	D	F	G	E	H
```
Resampling along columns would interpolate between f0 contours that are not meant to be contiguous, and I don't see a clean way to align contiguous contours.

Atm, I'm planning to resample using a nearest neighbor interpolation to avoid this problem. It's certainly not ideal, but I don't see a cleaner way. Thoughts?

> My $2e-2 extra: if this corner case is hitting, those empty reference annotations should be removed from the evaluation anyway.

My argument was only about the first case of the code (setting precision=1 when the set of predicted boundaries is zero). I agree that the second case (setting recall=1 when the set of reference boundaries is zero) should probably raise a warning instead.

> More precisely, we'd have to compute `lim_{tp, pp -> 0} pp/tp`, which is not a well-behaved sequence

For the precision, it's `lim_{tp, fp -> 0} tp/(tp+fp)`. And now I see the flaw in my reasoning. I assumed that `fp` would become zero first (the case of increasing a peak-picking threshold on a good novelty curve), so you'd end up with `lim_{tp -> 0} tp/tp`, which is 1. However, for a bad peak-picking curve, `tp` will become zero first, and we end up with `lim_{fp -> 0} 0/fp`, which is 0. Both is possible, so it doesn't matter which one you choose. Depending on the quality of your boundary detection algorithm, one or the other will give a more reasonable precision/recall curve.

Hey @darkblaze69 thanks for your interest.

# Bug 1

> `bitmath.parse_string('7.5KB')`
> `ValueError: The unit KB is not a valid bitmath unit`

This is not a bug. `KB` is not a valid unit in the **SI** or the **NIST** system. See the [Available Classes](http://bitmath.readthedocs.io/en/latest/classes.html#available-classes) and [Appendix - On Units](http://bitmath.readthedocs.io/en/latest/appendices.html#on-units) sections of the docs for more information on supported units. There is an additional note in the *Available Classes* section clarifying the `kB` vs `KB` situation.

* Nothing to fix here

# Bug 2

> Please add a fix to parse strings with only SI units. e.g. 10K, 10M etc.

I'm not so sure about this request. I'm fundamentally torn on the idea. At its heart bitmath is a library intended to finally do units **right**. The allowed inputs are strict and the output is always accurate and predictable.

Extending `parse_string` to handle dirty shell units would introduce ambiguity. There is no hard and fast rule that says `10M == 10000000 Bytes` **or that** `10M == 10485760`. The unix suffix `M` has no value without context (**B** or **b**). 

On the other hand, it might be useful to introduce a new function (or find a way to extend `parse_string` without breaking current behavior) that is specifically intended for parsing *dirty* or *fuzzy* units, like we often get in shell command output. Use of this function would be up to the user, and they'd have to know the inherent risks associated with it. For example, the resulting value may not be accurate. Depending on the shell command or config file value you could encounter a myriad of output formats.

* 10k (not real)
* 10K (not real)
* 10Ki (not real)
* 10KB (not real)
* 10kB (real)
* 10KiB (real)

Maybe I can come up with something that meets your requirements...

Hello,

Thanks, I didn't see this announcement.

Although, according to the [acme draft 12,](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-12#section-7.1.6) I think we shouldn't check the ready state here:

>  Order objects are created in the "pending" state.  Once all of the
   authorizations listed in the order object are in the "valid" state,
   the order transitions to the "ready" state.  **The order moves to the
   "processing" state after the client submits a request to the order's
   "finalize" URL** and the CA begins the issuance process for the
   certificate.  Once the certificate is issued, the order enters the
   "valid" state.

As you can see in my emphasis, the RFC explains the state of the order object has to be "processing" after the client has called the finalize URL.

Hence, IMHO, the patch proposed will be harmful, because it will let the client in an infinite loop of "sleep(2)" if the order is in the "ready" state instead of "processing" at this moment. Indeed, just before, the client calls the finalize URL with the CSR.

According to the new RFC, I think it will even be better to remove the "pending" status from this line, for the same reason: if the order status is "pending" or "ready" at this time, there's nothing that will change its state and so the client will just loop on "sleep(2)" for ever.

import numpy as np
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt

from vis.utils import utils
from vis.utils.vggnet import VGG16
from vis.visualization import visualize_activation
import keras


def visualize_random(num_categories=10, show=True):
    """Example to show how to visualize multiple filters via activation maximization.
    Args:
        num_categories: The number of random categories to visualize. (Default Value = 5)
    """
    # Build the VGG16 network with ImageNet weights
    model = VGG16(weights='imagenet', include_top=True)
    print('Model loaded.')

    # The name of the layer we want to visualize
    # (see model definition in vggnet.py)
    layer_name = 'predictions'
    layer_idx = [idx for idx, layer in enumerate(model.layers) if layer.name == layer_name][0]

    # Visualize couple random categories from imagenet.
    indices = np.random.permutation(1000)[:num_categories]
    images = []
    for idx in indices:
        img = visualize_activation(model, layer_idx, filter_indices=idx, max_iter=500)
        img = utils.draw_text(img, utils.get_imagenet_label(idx))
        images.append(img)

    # Easily stitch images via `utils.stitch_images`
    stitched = utils.stitch_images(images)
    if show:
        plt.axis('off')
        plt.imshow(stitched)
        plt.title('Random imagenet categories')
        plt.show()


def visualize_multiple_same_filter(num_runs=3, show=True):
    """Example to show how to visualize same filter multiple times via different runs.
    Args:
        num_runs: The number of times the same filter is visualized
    """
    # Build the VGG16 network with ImageNet weights
    model = VGG16(weights='imagenet', include_top=True)
    print('Model loaded.')

    # The name of the layer we want to visualize
    # (see model definition in vggnet.py)
    layer_name = 'predictions'
    layer_idx = [idx for idx, layer in enumerate(model.layers) if layer.name == layer_name][0]

    # 20 is the imagenet category for 'ouzel'
    indices = [20] * num_runs
    images = []
    for idx in indices:
        img = visualize_activation(model, layer_idx, filter_indices=idx, max_iter=500)
        img = utils.draw_text(img, utils.get_imagenet_label(idx))
        images.append(img)

    # Easily stitch images via `utils.stitch_images`
    stitched = utils.stitch_images(images)
    if show:
        plt.axis('off')
        plt.imshow(stitched)
        plt.title('Multiple runs of ouzel')
        plt.show()


def visualize_multiple_categories(show=True):
    """Example to show how to visualize images that activate multiple categories
    """
    # Build the VGG16 network with ImageNet weights
    model = VGG16(weights='imagenet', include_top=True)
    print('Model loaded.')

    # The name of the layer we want to visualize
    # (see model definition in vggnet.py)
    layer_name = 'predictions'
    layer_idx = [idx for idx, layer in enumerate(model.layers) if layer.name == layer_name][0]

    # Visualize [20] (ouzel) and [20, 71] (An ouzel-scorpion :D)
    indices = [20, [20, 71]]
    images = []
    for idx in indices:
        img = visualize_activation(model, layer_idx, filter_indices=idx, max_iter=500)
        img = utils.draw_text(img, utils.get_imagenet_label(idx))
        images.append(img)

    # Easily stitch images via `utils.stitch_images`
    stitched = utils.stitch_images(images)
    if show:
        plt.axis('off')
        plt.imshow(stitched)
        plt.title('Multiple category visualization')
        plt.show()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    print (keras.__version__)
    print('Visualizing random imagenet output categories')
    visualize_random(3)

    print('Visualizing same filter over multiple runs')
    visualize_multiple_same_filter()

    print('Visualizing multiple categories')
    visualize_multiple_categories()

Great point on leave_inner! Github readme updated with some examples.

Progress bars will display correctly in normal for loops but putting them within other TQDM loops means that you can get an ETA for the whole process and nicely nested loops. TQDM cuts out all of that work regarding timing your code and estimating the ETA.

If you're training several models for many epochs in a notebook, probably best to use tqdm_notebook(leave=True) for your outer loop, and TQDMNotebookCallback(leave_inner=False, leave_outer=False) for training.

That should give you a total of 3 progress bars at any time: each model, each epoch, each batch.

Updates in the master branch on github. Haven't pushed to PyPI yet.

* Default is now leave_inner=False for the notebook. Readme shows an example with false and true.
* Additional options show_inner and show_outer can be used to suppress one or the other bars entirely.
* Updated readme that using TQDMCallback withing tqdm for loops isn't required but adds in some nice looking nested progress bars.

Cheers,
Ben

I modified your code so it can properly close the stream. The following works in a Python console...

    def streamTweet(api):
        r = api.request('user')
        api.stream = r
        try:
            for item in r:
                print(item['text'] if 'text' in item else item)
        except AttributeError:
            print('***CLOSED***')

    t = threading.Thread(target=streamTweet, args=(api,))
    t.start()

Then close the stream with this...

    def closeStream(api):
        print('***CLOSING***')
        api.stream.response.raw.close()

    t = threading.Thread(target=closeStream, args=(api,))
    t.start()

It takes about 30 seconds to close, but it happens in the background.

For some reason `closeStream()` blocks with Python 3 and doesn't block with Python 2. The above code works with either. Though, if you are using Python 2 it is not necessary to call `closeStream()` in a thread. You can simply call:

    closeStream(api)

Your ``28/08/2015 16:14:59.001,21536,21536,21536,21536,1`` bar is obviously not past your end of session time. When this bar comes in a because it has crossed no resampling boundary, the method returns "False"

The next invocation puts ``sessend`` in the context time of the new bar (this "in the context" avoids precision errors with the coding/decoding to/from float/datetime), but the new bar is already in a new session/day. And the old bar is missed.

Actually the 1st output bar of the next day

```31/08/2015 09:15:59.000000, 21522, 21536,21450,21455,8```

has amongst others the open price of this other bar

```28/08/2015 16:14:00.000,21522,21522,21522,21522,1```

In this case the correction must apply to ``_barupdate`` which must only allow data within the defined session times (the default is the entire day) and then ``_barisover_minutes_sub`` checking if the next incoming data has surpassed the defined end of the session.

The beginning of ``_barupdate`` looks like:

```python
    def _barupdate(self, index=0, replaying=False):

```python
    def _barisover_minutes_sub(self, index):

Oh, no worries; I've been terribly busy, too -- I think the rest of your suggestions are pretty reasonable, except that I think we really do need to keep `--delete-from-keychain` around.  On some platforms -- ones that do not have a default system keychain -- the keychain library will write to an encrypted file in some place that might not be familiar to the user, and even in situations in which the user is on a platform having a system keyring, most people have no idea how their keychain works or how to access/alter/delete those entries.  I'd hate to force people to figure out how to manipulate either their system keychain, or locate some random encrypted file in the event that they decide to change their MyFitnessPal password.

Setting all of that aside; a lot of the strangeness in the way we interact with the command-line client is rooted in our using combinations of keyword options rather than actual subcommands, but that ship sailed some time ago, and would need a major version bump and some effort for getting us back to a sane place.

The bindings file that you have in the archive does not have the the "/data/customer" list that you are trying to deserialise to defined within it. 

```
[~/Downloads/testpyangbind_epnm]
(11:27 - s008) piccolo> grep "data" cisco_customer.py
```

There are also missing dependencies to be able to regenerate the bindings (imported YANG modules) so I can't validate the loads call for you. However, it looks like you have the name of the class that you want to deserialise to defined incorrectly. If you are not using `--split-class-dir` then the name of the container is re-mapped so that it is unique, so for example if I were to want to deserialise just the `customer` JSON that you supplied then this would be done via:

```
import pyangbind.lib.pybindJSON as pbj
import cisco_customer

obj = """{
"description": "default customer",
"name": "Infrastructure",
"fdn": "MD=CISCO_EPNM!CUSTOMER=Infrastructure"
}"""

x = pbj.loads(obj, cisco_customer, "yc_customer_cisco_customer__customer")

print x.get(filter=True)
```

`yc_customer_cisco_customer__customer` is the mapped name of object which is included in the `/data/customer` list. If `--split-class-dir` is used, then equally you can import to the customer class from the `bindings.data.customer` module.

HTH,

OK. So my first specific suggestion is that you're using flatmap a lot. This is probably the source of a lot of your performance issues. Its performance isn't *terrible* but it will typically be significantly worse than trying to produce the same thing without flatmap.

One specific instance of this is that you're using s.flatmap(lambda x: just(f(x))) a lot. This is the same as s.map(f), which should work a lot better.

As mentioned on IRC, you definitely shouldn't be doing just(choice(...))). This is basically guaranteed to cause bugs. That's what sampled\_from is for.

Modern SQLAlchemy doesn't emphasise bound metadata, so it should be explicit when you need to pass the engine. You can get the actual engine using `engine._engine`:

```python
metadata = MetaData(...)
metadata.reflect(bind=engine._engine)

...


table = Table(..., autoload_with=engine._engine, extend_existing=True)
```

These will block, so you can run them within an executor, something like this:

```
def blocking_fn():
    metadata.reflect(bind=engine._engine)

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_in_executor(None, blocking_fn)
```

Am I correct that there would never be a need to mix token-based and text-based matching in the same Grammar? If so, we could just have a subclass called ListGrammar that would emit a slightly different salad of Expressions: TokenExpressions in the place of Literals, for example. That would save the dispatch between basestrings and lists, improving performance and decoupling the new functionality from the existing code. I'm toying with even reusing the Literal syntax for tokens, since there would be no need for it in a token-oriented grammar. (That has the additional bonus of not having to wait for the grammar composition stuff from #30.) What do you guys think of this sketch?

```python
grammar = ListGrammar(r"""
    defun = "DEF" "SYMBOL" "LPAR" ("SYMBOL" ("COMMA" "SYMBOL")*) "RPAR" "COLON"
            "NEWLINE" "INDENT" block "DEDENT"
    block = (statement "NEWLINE")+
    statement = ...
    """)
grammar.parse([Token('DEF'), ...])
```

If you want to whitelist, @mvcisback's solution is the thing. But if you want to blacklist, I think the ultimate solution is to make a subgrammar (once #30 is implemented) and override the rules you hate with a rule that throws an explicit error. I eventually want to implement "naught ORs" in Parsimonious to improve error reporting, for just such situations as yours.

Excellent Thanks I'm dealing with the indentation issue as well.  Turns out the token grammar allows me to create a more complex structure after creating a simple structure using regex to create tokens.

in case anyone else wants to know how to do this.  Here's the getting started sample done with the TokenGrammar.
```python
>>> from parsimonious.utils import Token
>>> from parsimonious import TokenGrammar
>>> grammar = TokenGrammar("""
... bold_text = bold_open text bold_close
... text = "TEXT"
... bold_open = "BOLD_OPEN"
... bold_close = "BOLD_CLOSE"
... """)
>>> tl = []
>>> tl.append(Token(type='BOLD_OPEN'))
>>> tl.append(Token(type='TEXT'))
>>> tl.append(Token(type='BOLD_CLOSE'))
```

```python
>>> print(grammar.parse(tl))
<Node called "bold_text" matching "[<Token "BOLD_OPEN">, <Token "TEXT">, <Token "BOLD_CLOSE">]">
    <Node called "bold_open" matching "[<Token "BOLD_OPEN">]">
    <Node called "text" matching "[<Token "TEXT">]">
    <Node called "bold_close" matching "[<Token "BOLD_CLOSE">]">
```

Hm.. each websocket connection is dedicated to a single Wormhole instance. If there are multiple connections, they need to use separate connections. (I'll agree this is wasteful if you're doing a lot of simultaneous connections, but I consider that an unusual use case, and the protocol simplicity of one-connection-per-wormhole is compelling).

The `id` field is intended for debugging and logging/timing/tracing, not for operational use. The main purpose is for the timing diagram tools (`misc/dump-timing.py`).

If we want to enable sharing a single connection for multiple wormholes.. hm, I think we'd need to invent another field (maybe `wormhole_id`?), and use *that* to decide which `Wormhole` object each response should get routed to. I'll agree that we could probable re-use `id` for this, if we were more deliberate about how it gets used. I'm up for fixing the other issues you mentioned, if only for completeness/consistency.

The requests that have RPC-like semantics are still handled by a state machine: there's no table of pending requests (like the way Foolscap would do it). Instead, there's a state machine that's now in the "I want a response" state, and if/when a response arrives, that'll move it to the "I no longer care about responses" state (and deliver some event to some higher-level state machine). This helps us with connection re-establishment: if the websocket connection is dropped and then reestablished while we're in the "I want a response" state, the request will be re-transmitted (since we don't know whether the connection dropped before or after the request got delivered).

It looks like modern Twisted tests for `service_identity` as soon as `twisted.internet.endpoints` is imported, which magic-wormhole does all the time. So even though we aren't yet using TLS, we need it installed to avoid the warning.

I guess I'll change the twisted dependency to be `twisted[tls]`, which will pull in service-identity (or whatever other libraries Twisted decides it needs in the future). That will pull in a bunch of new requirements (some ASN1 modules, `pyopenssl`, and `cryptography` which has binary components), which is a drag. But I guess we'll be moving the rendezvous server connection to TLS eventually, just on general principles, so we might as well bite the dependency bullet now.

This is tricky. The general principal I've tried to go for with django-require is to expose as little of the requirejs functionality into Django. That is, if it can be configured using a build profile, it should be. That allows new features to be added to requirejs without bloating the django-require API.

I'd be tempted to say that this falls under that category of "should be configured in RequireJS". The moment you want to configure the build further, you're going to need per-module build profiles anyway.

Just a thought, but I'd almost always recommend a single monolithic main.js file across the site, and implement per-page init functions within it. That way, you only pay the cost of a single JS download on the first page load, and the rest of the pages of your site can used the cached version. Sure, the initial page load is slightly longer, but the payoff of never having to load more JS is huge. It also makes build configuration a lot easier!

@goldmann True, the entire point of docker-squash is rewriting history. :) However, I guess the issue is that I was expecting docker-squash to only alter history for the layers it is squashing.

When using the `-f` flag (as in the example above), docker-squash only squashes a subset of all the layers forming my image, yet in the history the layers which were not squashed are still being "altered". In my minimal test case, I'm using `alpine:latest` as my base, and add two additional layers (two RUN commands). The image history contains three layers: The original alpine image (which happens to have a single layer) and the two layers I've added which a single file each.

Then, I ask docker-squash to *only* squash those last two layers together. So, the new image only has two layers: The original alpine layer, which I expect to be completely unmodified, and a single layer which contains the two files I've created. However, that original alpine layer *is* modified: In `docker history` we suddenly lost the image ID. While it is still the exact same alpine layer, which is even reflected by its created date.

Of course, when using images which consist more layers than just the single layer in this minimal example, the change becomes more apparent.

So it's not that I find it unexpected that the docker history is modified, but just that it loses the image id's for layers I expected to remain unaltered. :)

>  If we give them two functions for same behavior mostly people will randomly use one

I guess this is a good point. I feel the problem is, that there are different ways you can use google spreadsheets in applications. 

One (and I think the most common) is to just have it as data store. So just load data from/save data to it, but not actually use any formatting features or special API calls. The primary concern here is speed and ease of access in different ways (by row, by column, list row-dictionary, entire sheet) and then put the data back once processed. Such a use will also usually just use a single sheet and mostly use a very simple spreadsheet and a simple worksheet model. No need for data ranges or cells. Python dictionaries and lists are much better for storing and handling this kind of data.

A second way is to actually use spreadsheet features and structures. To then better display it in the spreadsheet, use formulas etc. This use needs a way to handle synchronization and good integration with the format properties. And I feel most parts of the module are aimed at this.

A third way would be to not have any data locally, but actually let the spreadsheet handle all the operations. I am not sure if anyone actually does this, but I guess this would be useful for cases where you don't have a lot of computing power locally or need advanced sheets features and do a lot of computations and charts within sheets.

Each need their own model to work well, because currently this module is a bit of a mix of 1 and 2 (which makes a lot of sense), but it would be nicer if we could better separate this. 

It would be possible to do this by having different classes for each model and a common base class. 

For example have a BaseSpreadsheet, SimpleSpreadsheet, Spreadsheet (same as current basically), RemoteSpreadsheet (for requests only). Then people could choose what they want.

Of course to start it slow I would start with creating a SimpleSpreadsheet which supports all the row/col get & update functions, read the entire sheet and have a col/row argument, but nothing more. Then it would also be easier to implement the DictReaderSpreadsheet.

see [this](https://developers.google.com/sheets/api/reference/rest/v4/spreadsheets#ExtendedValue) and [this](https://developers.google.com/sheets/api/reference/rest/v4/DateTimeRenderOption#ENUM_VALUES.SERIAL_NUMBER).
In short 

> The whole number portion of the value (left of the decimal) counts the days since December 30th 1899.

So, I've finally found some time and motivation to look into this. My conclusion - going of the tests - is that the documentation was wrong, so I've updated that. As for what you actually want, I'm a bit confused:

1. Why do you want to map labels to values? The intended usage is doing something like `mymodel.choice = Choices.some_attribute`, which assigns the correct value. Human-readable label is derived from the value.

2. There is actually a risk - if you're using (translatable) labels, you may end up having the same human readable label for different values. It's not possible to do the reverse mapping in that case. While having the same _representation_ of different values is an odd thing in itself, it _may_ be a valid use case in simplified UX-es.

So, I'm not yet building something into the library to get this reverse mapping, until it's clear what would be the actual use case for it. Could you provide some more context?

Hi,
the way to go is to use the controller public method [``slice(startIndex, numberOfItem)``](https://github.com/lorenzofox3/Smart-Table/blob/master/src/stTable.js#L137). The selectPage private function of the pagination directive simply calculate the offset index and numbOfItem to provide to ctrl.slice based on the directive configuration.

For the second part of your problem, if you want to locate your new row in the currently "extended" displayed collection, you have the controller public method [getFilteredCollection ](https://github.com/lorenzofox3/Smart-Table/blob/master/src/stTable.js#L151). I say "extended ", because the actual displayed collection only contains the currently displayed page whereas the collection you'll get form getFilteredCollection will return you the result of filter and sort operations based on the current tableState. As the references are the same in every collection, you can do: 
```
ctrl.getFilteredCollection().indexOf(myNewRow)
```
to get the offset to pass to the slice function so that your new item is for example the first item of your page

We ran into this issue; the cause was, as seems to be mentioned above, that we imported an old database which hadn't been migrated past `python-social-auth==0.2.21` and tried to use it with a codebase using `social-auth-app-django==1.2.0`  and `social-auth-core==1.4.0`

We solved it by these steps:
1) `pip install python-social-auth==0.2.21` 
2) replace  `social_django` with `social.apps.django_app.default` in `INSTALLED_APPS`
3) `python manage.py migrate`
4) restore `social_django` instead of `social.apps.django_app.default` in `INSTALLED_APPS` 
5) `pip uninstall python-social-auth`
6) `python manage.py migrate`

I hope this helps someone else.

Hello, It seems that Twitter has two types of geo-information in the user profile and the tweet. In the Profile, there is only the text of the place at `tweet.location` like this: ```python { ... 'location': 'Mountain View', ... } ``` If the user enables geo-information of the tweet, you can get it as the `tweet.place` value of tweet response from API. ```python { ... 'in_reply_to_user_id_str': '12371162', 'is_quote_status': False, 'lang': 'en', 'place': {'attributes': {}, 'bounding_box': {'coordinates': [[[139.995787, 35.947444], [140.173305, 35.947444], [140.173305, 36.236812], [139.995787, 36.236812]]], 'type': 'Polygon'}, 'contained_within': [], 'country': '日本', 'country_code': 'JP', 'full_name': '茨城 つくば市', 'id': '24f07b6d792fa678', 'name': 'つくば市', 'place_type': 'city', 'url': 'https://api.twitter.com/1.1/geo/id/24f07b6d792fa678.json'}, 'retweet_count': 0, 'retweeted': False, 'source': 'Twitter Web Client', ... } ``` from [my test tweet](https://twitter.com/__shuuji3/status/943563689604427776). But if a user turn off geo embed, we cannot get place so a response will be like this: ```python { ... 'in_reply_to_user_id_str': '12371162', 'is_quote_status': False, 'lang': 'en', 'place': None ... } ``` You can find the [detail information of geo object here](https://developer.twitter.com/en/docs/tweets/data-dictionary/overview/geo-objects).

No problem! So, I stand corrected from my previous post. As per the README:
```

 # If you need more complex filtering, filter() also accepts a Python-like search expression:
#
# items = my_folder.filter(
#       "start < '2016-01-02T03:04:05T' and end > '2016-01-01T03:04:05T' and categories in ('foo', 'bar')"
```

So, you can in fact perform more complex searches within the subject and body of emails. 

Example: 

```
items = account.inbox.filter("subject in 'dog'")      #returns all emails that contain the emails with dog in the subject line

items = account.inbox.filter("body in 'dog'")      #returns all emails that contain the emails with dog in the body of the email
```

We're using `__truediv__` to support this syntax, which mimics the syntax of folder navigation that `pathlib` uses. Internally, it returns any folder in `account.inbox.children` with the name `test-abc` and throws `ErrorFolderNotFound` if there is no match.

I forgot to load the image first. I tried it like so:

```python
image = face_recognition.load_image_file(data)

But that gave an `embedded null byte` error. I then wrapped the `data` variable in a `BytesIO` call, and that did it

```python
from io import BytesIO

sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_UNIX, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
sock.connect('/path/to/socket')
data = False

while True:
	buf = sock.recv(4096)

	if not buf:
		break

	if not data:
		data = buf
	else:
		data = data + buf

image = face_recognition.load_image_file(BytesIO(data))
face_locations = face_recognition.face_locations(image)
face_encodings = face_recognition.face_encodings(image, face_locations)

```

One other thing I want to mention, unrelated to the discrepancies, I would be careful with how your model is set up. How your categories are coded in your model assume a linear relationship of the HR between categories. Here is a simple example, image your categorical variable is education level; primary only (0), secondary (1), and graduate (2). With how you have the model implemented, the model assumes that the HR between primary and secondary is the same as the HR between secondary and graduate. This may not be true. It would be better practice to allow for non-linearity by coding the categorical variable as a dummy variable (two binary variables, with one indicating a value of 1, and one indicating a value of 2). You can use ```pd.get_dummies``` to do this.

I have searched for the old issues and found that the author has explained [here]() in #141 that the constructor and ```init_app``` are not meant to be used together. This is for initialize many application with same ```Security``` instance. So for fixing this issue is just adding ```self._state = None``` as followings.

```python
if app is not None and datastore is not None:
    self._state = self.init_app(app, datastore, **kwargs)
else:
    self._state = None
```

And when users need to access security attributes, use ```state``` object returned by ```init_app```. Here is an example on how to initialize using factory method.

```python
from celery import Celery
from flask import Flask
from flask_security import Security, .. datastore stuff ..
from flask_mail import Mail

celery = Celery()
mail = Mail()
security = Security()

def create_app():
    """Initialize Flask instance."""
    app = Flask(__name__)
    mail.init_app(app)
    security_ctx = security.init_app(app,..datastore stuff..)

    @security_ctx.send_mail_task
    def delay_flask_security_mail(msg):
        send_flask_mail.delay(msg)

    return app
```

The confusion for me is in this statement:

> The result of this is that the cookie will not expire [...]

In Chrome, these are being set as Session cookies and are deleted when the browser is closed. Per the flask documentation, if you do not set a max_age parameter the default is to create a session cookie. (see http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/api/#flask.Response.set_cookie). 

Here are the cookies as seen by Chrome:

![screen shot 2017-04-04 at 3 18 48 pm](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/57712/24681519/6e76498a-194a-11e7-95f5-90849431b846.png)

If the intention is for the cookies to "never" expire, they will need a far future expiration date (see: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3290424/set-a-cookie-to-never-expire).

Hopefully this makes sense, let me know if I am missing something. I would be happy to submit a pull request but I want to make sure I am not missing something here. Thanks!

My guess would be, one of your `INSTALLED_APPS` has an `__init__.py` that's causing a `django.db.models` import.
In the noteback case, the traceback seems to indicate it's not doing a setup of the models prior to importing `django.db.models` - so mid-way through that import, it imports settings, runs through autoconfig, and something then tries to import the half loaded `django.db.models`..

A reasonable workaround: find whichever app it is and add it to `AUTOCONFIG_DISABLED_APPS`

The root cause of this is really that we're naively importing apps to find out if `<app>/autoconfig.py` exists. If we could be a bit smarter there and figure it out without needing to do a real import, it would solve a lot of these issues...
I'll have another think about that.

One gunicorn worker can handle hundreds/thousands of clients when using eventlet or gevent. If that is within your needs, then you should not worry about multiple processes.

If you do need to use multiple processes, then the documentation covers how to configure a message queue and a load balancer.

Well, this might be all water under the bridge if you are unable to run more tests, but what you are doing is actually not a great idea. The event handlers for a given client are serialized, to avoid potential race conditions or other side effects if two handlers for the same user are running in parallel. So sleeping for 90 seconds in an event handler could actually block other events for that client.

The eventlet and gevent async modes have a mode in which you can turn off this protection and allow handlers to run in parallel. I have it in my task list to implement the same thing for asyncio.

MkDocs supports an object for the `theme` property. See http://www.mkdocs.org/user-guide/configuration/#theme

Since the `theme` property in `pydocmd.yml` is copied to the `mkdocs.yml` configuration as-is, you can simply use the same type of object in your pydoc-markdown configuration.

```yaml
# pydocmd.yaml
theme:
  name: null
  custom_dir: mycustomtheme/
```

hey guys, sorry I'm a bit late here, but would it actually be possible to make some sort of range function for this? for example, here is a monkey patch that I made to my local version of the code to address this problem:

```python
class TimeRangeExternalTaskSensor(ExternalTaskSensor):
    """
    Monkey patch ExternalTaskSensor to enable fuzzy date searching; this is a hack
    :param execution_date_tolerance_before: how long before the target execution time to search for a task, in seconds
    :type execution_date_tolerance_before: int
    :param execution_date_tolerance_after: how long after the target execution time to search for a task, in seconds
    :type execution_date_tolerance_after: int
    """

    def __init__(
            self,
            execution_date_tolerance_before=0,
            execution_date_tolerance_after=0,
            *args, **kwargs):
        super(TimeRangeExternalTaskSensor, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.execution_date_tolerance_before = execution_date_tolerance_before
        self.execution_date_tolerance_after = execution_date_tolerance_after

    def poke(self, context):
        if self.execution_delta:
            dttm = context['execution_date'] - self.execution_delta
        else:
            dttm = context['execution_date']

        logging.info(
            'Poking for '
            '{self.external_dag_id}.'
            '{self.external_task_id} on '
            '{dttm} ... '.format(**locals()))
        TI = TaskInstance

        execution_date_lower_bound = dttm - datetime.timedelta(seconds=self.execution_date_tolerance_before)
        execution_date_upper_bound = dttm + datetime.timedelta(seconds=self.execution_date_tolerance_after)
        logging.info('Searching for matching task(s) with execution_date between [{}, {}]...'.format(execution_date_lower_bound, execution_date_upper_bound))

        session = settings.Session()
        count = session.query(TI).filter(
            TI.dag_id == self.external_dag_id,
            TI.task_id == self.external_task_id,
            TI.state.in_(self.allowed_states),
            TI.execution_date >= execution_date_lower_bound,
            TI.execution_date <= execution_date_upper_bound
        ).count()
        session.commit()
        session.close()
        return count
```

What a colossal pain. I'd ask why Python can't agree on how text works but that would trivialize how really hard it is to get right (and Python is way better than it used to be at this).

We can fix that by wrapping the binary file in a unicode codec reader.

```
textstream = codecs.getreader('utf-8')(request.files['file'])
d = json.load(textstream)
```

Works in 3.5, also works in 3.6; 🤞 that it will work in 2.7.

We can also put the reader as a file level constant (no real preference on my end):

```python
UTF8_READER = codecs.getreader('utf-8')
...
d = json.load(UTF8_READER(request.files['file']))
```

If we want to support more than UTF-8, we could use [chardet](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/chardet) to determine the encoding but, in my opinion, that's overkill.
LGTM if tested (make sure adding this doesn't break other parts of the system)

I took another look today and changed my mind, `null_technical_500_response` will need to be modified to clean up the stacktrace. Django calls django_extensions's `null_technical_500_response` for each Middleware Mixin object in a stack (because it is overridden in runserver_plus.py). By the time an uncaught exception occurs in the django app there are typically 8-10 of these Mixins in the stack, hence all the six reraise calls on top of the original exception.

I don't think this is related to python 3's exception chaining as mentioned [here](https://github.com/gitcoinco/web/issues/1811#issuecomment-407133097). I think `null_technical_500_response` needs to be modified to return django_extensions's version of a response to be displayed, instead of raising an exception. Not sure how to do this yet, but should be able to figure it out next time I can look in a few days.

Nice. Well, that was actually rather easy!

At first, you should get the layout for the current content. The easiest way is to render ``plone.app.blocks.layoutbehavior.ContentLayoutView``. That's because the layout may be either in the content field or from a shared storage.

Then you should parse the resulting HTML with lxml.etree.fromstring and iterate through all the data-tile-attributes. There's XPath expression for that in ``plone.app.blocks.utils.bodyTileXPath``. You could look through the utils-module about how that's used.

Once you have the URLs from data-tile-attributes, you can get the tile view classes (with their data as its data-property/attribute) with restrictedTraverse.

> I'm still not clear how the pinns are sorted. A order where the later overides the former would be

Order is how they are in the extends chain, example, given in each a version of ``my.pkg`` was declared

1. buildout.cfg (3.0.3)
2  buildout.cfg extends foo.cfg (3.0.1)
3. buildout.cfg extends bar.cfg (2.0)
5. foo cfg. extends baz.cfg (3.1)
6. found a newer major version at pypi (4.0)
7. found a newer minor version at pypi (3.2)
8. found a newer major prerelease version at pypi (5.1b2)

The we get (after I fixed the bug above)::

    my.pkg
        3.0.3............... A buildout.cfg
        2.0 ................ I bar.cfg
        3.0.1 .............. I foo.cfg
        3.1 ................ I baz.cfg
        4.0 ................ U Major        
        3.2 ................ U Minor
        5.1b2............... P Majorpre

I think I'll put this into the README. For me this is the most logical way to analyse what happens in my buildout.

Suggestion of text:

---
scripts

Add this parameter with no arguments to suppress script generation. Otherwise (i.e. without this parameter), scripts for packages added to the eggs parameter will be generated. You may also configure per script. E.g.:

```
[instance]
recipe = plone.recipe.zope2instance
eggs =
  Plone
  mr.migrator
  my.package
scripts = my_package_script
```

In the above example, only the `my_package_script` will be generated. Keep in mind that the egg containing the script (`my.package` in the example) must be listed explicitly in the `eggs` option, even if it's a dependency of an already listed egg.
---

I think we are filling the namespace of objects too much. Every public method/attribute provided by HasTraits has a real cost to the quality of the APIs of classes that inherit from it, so we have to carefully consider any new method name. We should especially avoid taking up generic not-traitlets-specific names (like `notify`), because they mean that classes using traitlets cannot use those method names.

A better name would probably be `notify_trait`, but I think that was already taken and is now deprecated. It is better than either `notify` or `notify_change`, though.

Another question: Should the notify methods even be public? Shouldn't these only be called internally on change events?
For a validator, better names would be "current" and "proposed", rather than "old" and "new".

This is NOT added to parser, instead achieved as using the AutoStructify
Transformers (see doc), which could be disabled.  The parser remains as
plain good commonmark parser, which means all the syntax are commonmark
compatible.

The syntax is adhere to commonmark spec in a sense that this is basically
literal blocks, and they will be rendered as literal blocks with $ sign in
worst case when the option is not enabled.
The general idea of AutoStructify is to adhere to commonmark but doing
something smart to find structure component  in the code and structify it,
so you get some capability of rst out of markdown.

Yeah, when you run `myscript.py` as a script, its module name is `__main__`; when you run `monkeytype stub myscript`, its module name is `myscript`. It may be possible to hack around this by mangling the name of traces recorded with module `__main__` (if we can figure out the actual module name for the `__main__` module, which might not be easy to do reliably). I have mixed feelings about it; monkeytype needs to import the modules it annotates, and importing a script that isn't carefully written could have unintended side effects. I think I'd rather just have MonkeyType clearly intended for annotating the modules used by the script; if you want to annotate `myscript.py` itself, treat it as a module and make another short script that imports and calls it, and run monkeytype with that script.

For now I'll add a documentation note to that effect. But I'll leave this open as a low-pri enhancement; if someone submits a PR that implements it in a way that looks robust, I'd consider it.

>  Using a lockfile from a different OS is fine if all your packages are modern and well-behaved (either platform-agnostic or have good cross-platform support). Otherwise Pipenv can do things wrong, like installing dependencies that don’t work, or not installing all needed dependencies.

Note that "`pipenv` with a `conda` backend" would be comparable to an arrangement along the lines of "`pipenv` with an `apt-get` backend": it's confusing different layers of the packaging stack in a way that doesn't make logistical sense.

However, the `conda` folks are open to the idea of transparently managing wheel archives from PyPI as if they were conda packages: https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/5202

That approach would better respect the layering described in http://www.curiousefficiency.org/posts/2016/09/python-packaging-ecosystem.html#platform-management-or-plugin-management, which points out that `pip` (and hence tools based on it, like `pipenv`) is a language specific plugin manager for *Python* runtimes, whereas tools like `conda` are language independent component managers for the construction of larger software systems.

Yes, I was thinking about this in my waking hours this morning. Ultimately, Python 3.4 (and earlier) will get old and all namespace packages will look like PEP420 namespace packages. When that happens, we'd like for setuptools and libraries like setuptools to have an interface that's clean and free of legacy cruft.

To get there without breaking existing packages, I believe we need a mechanism for the `find_packages` behavior to remain unchanged (for now), but signal that in future versions, the behavior will change.

Here's an idea. What if:

## phase 1

`find_packages` will invoke both finders and where the results differ, warn that in the future `find_packages` will return the larger set of packages and indicate that to retain existing behavior, use `find_packages` excluding the unwanted packages, but setuptools use the compatible set.

## phase 2

About 6 months later, as a backward-incompatible release, `find_packages` uses PEP420Finder.find.


I could imagine a 3-phase release that retains the legacy behavior, but the more I think about it, the less important that seems to retain.

Hi @youngpm, thanks for checking this in. I've actually seen behavior like this before in my local testing, but I've never gotten to the bottom of it. We've never seen it in production, so it hasn't been a super high priority to fix, but it's good to know I'm not the only one who's seen this. Pykafka only knows how to store offsets in kafka itself, so offsets in zookeeper should not be a factor here.

There are two things I recommend changing about your test script to see if/how they affect the results you're seeing. First, I'd use `auto_commit_enable=True` with `auto_commit_interval_ms=1000`. Committing the offset after each message as you're doing should work, but the tests I've run are all using autocommitting since it's slightly more performant in production. Second, I'd change `auto_offset_reset` to `OffsetType.LATEST`. It's possible that your consumer is resetting itself to the beginning of the topic because of this setting, so it would be good to remove that variable.

Looking more deeply into the `BalancedConsumer` code, I don't actually think there's any way to do what you're asking for with pykafka. An issue in the `BalancedConsumer` is that the [`_decide_partitions`](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L420) function doesn't know how to do anything other than use the standard balancing algorithm to get its partitions. Thus, if any consumer that wants to temporarily take hold of all partitions does so and triggers the watch functions of the other consumers in the group, those other consumers will still decide to keep their standard set of partitions, causing the overwriting issue you mentioned.

This problem also seems to apply to the `ManagedBalancedConsumer`, since it uses the same `_decide_partitions`, but it's possible this functionality would be easier to add to that class since the partition assignment is completely decided by a single consumer, the leader. If there was a way to guarantee that a given consumer would become the group leader, that could be used to set an overriding partition assignment like we're talking about here.

In the meantime, a workaround we use for this at Parsely is to redeploy the entire consumer group with the `reset_offset_on_start=True` flag.

Just to revive this discussion a bit: I had a couple of conversations at SciPy 2018 about this. I'll give one-sentence overviews, but hoping that the people I ping will chime in with more details about their ideas.

First I'd like to point out that we've moved to ImageIO for our IO in #3126. I don't know currently whether we toss out the input `imageio.Image` object and just keep the array, but either way, metadata input is now in skimage. I'm not sure about the output capabilities of ImageIO. @almarklein?

Second, @JDWarner proposed `meta=[dict]` and `return_meta=[True/False]` for returning the metadata. This is currently what I'm leaning towards myself.

But, third, as a counterpoint, @danielballan suggested that changing what is returned based on input values is a bad habit, and he's ok with scikit-image being the lowest-level layer in the image processing stack that others can build on (ie essentially the current status quo), and that if we want to support meta maybe we can do this in a parallel namespace (`skimage.meta.*`) that would *always* accept and return the metadata dictionary.

To be honest, although I am *very* sympathetic to the principles of type purity promoted by this proposal, the end result makes me unhappy. "Practicality beats purity", and a `.meta` namespace seems impractical to me. But I'm not ready to dismiss the idea outright.

The truth is that the Pythonic way to do this is indeed a more complex object that builds on NumPy arrays (e.g. XArrays), and/or duck-typing (e.g. adding a `.meta` attribute to whatever array-like we pass), but both of these are unpalatable for our library. I really think *the* main reason that skimage is where it is today is because of the simplicity and interoperability of working with NumPy arrays, and we absolutely must not jeopardise that.

Here are some tasks that I think could be useful in making the API clearer and more accessible:

1. **Naming conventions for srcOutput and destInput.** The current names like "topDownIn" and "temporalTopDown" are confusing and inconsistent. First, I think it would actually be clearer to remove defaults and require that every call to link has a srcOutput and a destInput. Then I would enforce a naming convention that requires the names to include both the region that they belong to and the direction that they will send data in (top down or bottom up). We will need to be sure that the names don't get too verbose though. Also as a side note, in making examples for the network api, it will be helpful to include several variations on srcOutput and destInput, such as one that has multiple regions connecting to the same srcOutput.

2. **Automatic generation of graphical representations of networks.** It might be helpful to create a function that takes in a network and creates a graphical representation, using Networkx for example, just to show all the regions and how they’re connected. We may want to wait for the serialization effort to be complete so that graphs are generated from serialized files, potentially through JS in a browser, but we could also go ahead and generate the graphs directly.

3. **Linking policies.** The only linking policy that is fully implemented and used is the Uniform one. There are a few other policies that are at various stages of development, including FanIn2Link, Split, and OneToOne. I'm not sure what these policies would be designed to do or if we want to continue their development. Regardless, it might make sense to make Uniform the default policy to help reduce the clutter in the link method.

4. **Efficient access to active columns/cells in a region.** It is currently a little difficult to access the active cells in a region at a given time. We may want to add a function at the network level that efficiently gives us this access without introducing inefficiencies.

5. **Demos of Network API.** It’s helpful for users trying to understand the network API that the demo corresponds to the hot gym example. Other demos that correspond to other existing examples, such as one involving geospatial encoding, would probably also be helpful.

6. **Prepackaging commonly grouped regions.** There are a number of regions that will often be grouped together and linked in the same way. For example, linking a RecordSensor region to a spatial pooler, and then often to a temporal memory region (as is done in the demo) will likely be common. As part of the API, It would be nice to be able to get a network already initialized with these regions and then be able to extend it as needed.

7. **Documentation.** Fleshing out the documentation for the API will of course be very helpful for making it more accessible.

Here is my daemon for listening to the WeMo switch, things are finally working nicely:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import logging, os, os.path, time, sys, gevent
from gevent               import socket
sys.path.insert(1, os.path.expanduser('~/src/ouimeaux'))
from ouimeaux.environment import Environment
from ouimeaux.signals     import statechange, receiver

def connect():
    global log, sock
    sock      = socket.socket(socket.AF_UNIX, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    connected = False

    while not connected:
        try:
            log.debug('connecting to hue daemon')
            sock.connect(os.path.expanduser('~/run/toggle_hue.sock'))
            connected = True
            log.debug('connection to hue daemon successful')
        except:
            log.error('connection to hue daemon failed, retrying')
            gevent.sleep(5)

def send(msg):
    global log, sock
    while True:
        try:
            log.debug("sending '%s' message to hue daemon" % msg)
            sock.sendall(msg + '\012')
            log.debug("message '%s' sent to hue daemon successfully" % msg)
            return
        except (socket.error, IOError):
            log.error('not connected to hue daemon, reconnecting')
            connect()

logging.basicConfig(format='%(asctime)s %(message)s')
log = logging.getLogger('wemo_switch_daemon')
log.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

log.debug('starting wemo switch daemon')

connect()

log.debug('retrieving wemo environment')

env = Environment()
env.start()
env.discover(5)

switch = env.get_switch('rafael_living_wemo')

@receiver(statechange, sender=switch)
def switch_toggle(*args, **kwargs):
    if kwargs['state'] == 1:
        log.debug('received switch ON state event')
        gevent.spawn(send, 'on')
    else:
        log.debug('received switch OFF state event')
        gevent.spawn(send, 'off')

try:
    log.debug('entering event loop')
    env.wait()
except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
    log.debug('exiting wemo switch daemon')
    exit(0)
```

I notice that those require `sudo: true`. It's [documented](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/ci-environment/#Virtualization-environments)  that `sudo: true` builds run on a different infrastructure (non container-based) which is substantially slower to boot (up to 52 seconds vs up to 6), and anecdotally, I seem to recall that the queue to even be able to start `sudo: true` builds tended to be substantially longer than the container-based environments (whether that's still true or not now, I can't say; maybe someone has more recent experience).  All of which boils down to taking much longer to get feedback from the CI on a push/PR to those repositories. And since the builds for an organization (zopefoundation) are effectively queued together, that potentially slows down feedback for other repositories too.

Adding the Mac builds already made it frequently take *substantially* longer (hours sometimes) to get Travis to "go green" for those projects...but that wasn't a *huge* concern because the Linux builds that covered all the different supported Python versions and PURE_PYTHON variants, etc, typically finished within a minute anyway, so an experienced contributor was already able to get the most useful feedback quickly. (And could then cancel the pending Mac builds to be able to start the next commit build in case of failure.) And I can see the benefit to Mac users for having pre-built wheels, so it seems worth the minor hassle.

But the benefit to Linux users looks less enticing to me, since Linux users most often already have compilers installed (or if they're doing production deployments have already solved the problem of not having compilers on web server machines, etc, by using their own egg caches/devpi/docker images/whatever). There are no complicated external dependencies to install for these libraries either, and downstream packagers don't rip them apart to undo bundled dependencies (as was the rationale for gevent). In some cases, we'd conceivably be *downgrading* the quality of the code that Linux users would be running, since manylinux1 wheels are built with a very old compiler.

All of which makes me roughly -0 on this, I guess: not a fan, but not opposed in the face of good arguments.

If we do this, though, I would suggest we turn on a few Travis settings for those repos to limit the queue damage: first, the new setting that makes it cancel old builds when new commits to a branch come in (good for making sure that only the most recent commit builds, typically after a quick bugfix), and potentially the setting that only PRs go to Travis, not branches (cuts the number of builds for a PR in half, and since the workflow is to never commit to master and *rarely* to do multiple pushes to a branch before opening a PR, *probably* not changing the feedback most of the time).

The tests are failing with this error:
```
  File "/home/travis/build/zopefoundation/Persistence/src/Persistence/tests/test_persistent.py", line 43, in add
    self.cache[obj._p_oid] = obj
  File "/home/travis/build/zopefoundation/Persistence/eggs/persistent-4.2.4.2-py3.4-linux-x86_64.egg/persistent/picklecache.py", line 111, in __setitem__
    raise TypeError("Cache values must be persistent objects.")
TypeError: Cache values must be persistent objects.
```

That was surprising because it indicates that the tests are using the pure-Python implementation of the PickleCache. But sure enough, [that's what they're importing](https://github.com/zopefoundation/Persistence/blob/master/src/Persistence/tests/test_persistent.py#L22) with `from persistent.picklecache import PickleCache`. That should be changed to `from persistent import PickleCache` to get the correct implementation.

This type checking and exception was added to the Python PickleCache way back [in 2015 and persistent 4.1.0](https://github.com/zopefoundation/persistent/commit/169c07a04a82110d0ef559a69a36ca035c52f1d5#diff-9349838838c54a85a15e0537529654b8R82), as part of the efforts to make the Python implementations behave more like the C implementation. The C implementation has [always enforced that type check](https://github.com/zopefoundation/persistent/blob/4.0.0/persistent/cPickleCache.c#L1033) AFAICS (well, or at least attempted to; the check is very poor for some reason).

So basically AFAICS the tests are broken not because of a regression in persistent but because they're legitimately wrong. They were getting away with bad behaviour before, but not any longer.

class CQScheduler(Scheduler):
    def __init__(self, executor):
        self.executor = executor

    def resourceOffers(self, driver, offers):
        '''
        this resource offer process repeats when tasks finish and new resources become free
        @todo: 
            - 
        @fixed
            - should Scheduler dispatch more than one jobs per call
            
        '''
        # this is where Bedrock will dispatch jobs
        filters = {'refuse_seconds': 0.01}

        offers_to_decline = []
        tasks = []
        offers_to_accept = []
        declineRest = False

        for offer in offers:
            
            if declineRest:
                offers_to_decline.append(offer.id)
                continue

            cpus = self.getResource(offer.resources, 'cpus')
            mem = self.getResource(offer.resources, 'mem')

            tasks = binpack_tasks(cpus, mem, offer, self.executor)

            if len(tasks) > 0:

### Utils:
```python
from rest_framework_jwt.serializers import *
class CustomJSONWebTokenSerializer(JSONWebTokenSerializer):
    """
    Serializer class used to validate a username and password.
    'username' is identified by the custom UserModel.USERNAME_FIELD.
    Returns a JSON Web Token that can be used to authenticate later calls.
    """
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        Dynamically add the USERNAME_FIELD to self.fields.
        """
        super(JSONWebTokenSerializer, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

        self.fields[self.username_field] = serializers.CharField()
        self.fields['password'] = PasswordField(write_only=True)
        self.fields['tdvalue'] = serializers.IntegerField(required=False)

    def validate(self, attrs):
        credentials = {
            self.username_field: attrs.get(self.username_field),
            'password': attrs.get('password')
        }

        if all(credentials.values()):
            user = authenticate(**credentials)

            if user:
                if not user.is_active:
                    msg = _('User account is disabled.')
                    raise serializers.ValidationError(msg)

                tmp_exp = api_settings.JWT_EXPIRATION_DELTA

                if attrs.get('tdvalue') is not None:
                    api_settings.JWT_EXPIRATION_DELTA = timedelta(seconds=attrs.get('tdvalue'))

                payload = jwt_payload_handler(user)
                return {
                    'token': jwt_encode_handler(payload),
                    'user': user
                }
            else:
                msg = _('Unable to log in with provided credentials.')
                raise serializers.ValidationError(msg)
        else:
            msg = _('Must include "{username_field}" and "password".')
            msg = msg.format(username_field=self.username_field)
            raise serializers.ValidationError(msg)
```

The issue is resolved by modifying `populate_args` in `jnius_conversion.pxi`, such that the input `py_str` is always converted with `.encode('utf-8')` before calling `NewStringUTF`. This means only running the code under `except (UnicodeEncodeError, TypeError):` so that there's only one possible call to `NewStringUTF`.
The test drivers pass with this change with python 2 and 3. There may be some case not covered by them, but calling a string conversion routine twice to see which one works is a bad code smell, because otherwise you end up with the "Bush hid the facts" bug (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bush_hid_the_facts).

I think I've tracked this down to the (good!) change that happened between 6.0.0 and 6.1.0:

> fix registration of hooks for Django

PR:
https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/pull/966

Original issue:
https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/issues/884

Since [install_sys_hook](https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/f9e981e9850b6ed8e8c94d8507e65a2dcd3f691f/raven/base.py#L269) is working correctly, exceptions raised within a `manage.py|django-admin.py` session are handled by Sentry.

Here is a basic workaround I've settled on (YMMV):

```python
# in settings.py
import sys
SHELL = "shell" in sys.argv
...
RAVEN_CONFIG = {
    "ignore_exceptions": ("*") if SHELL else ()
}
```

In [should_capture](https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/f9e981e9850b6ed8e8c94d8507e65a2dcd3f691f/raven/base.py#L820), the wildcard`"*"` will match any exception.

Other `manage.py` commands that don't have a "shell" argument will continue to send exceptions to Sentry.
