Hi all,  I wanted to give a brief report of some of the things I've been investigating for this issue and some of the surprises/problems that have emerged.

The second kick profile derives from a truncated von Karman phase fluctuation power spectrum, which is related to a Kolmogorov phase power spectrum but generalized by 1) the presence of a finite outer scale (the von Karman bit), and 2) a low-k cutoff (the truncation bit).  In the quick-and-dirty version of the SecondKick code I had previously been working on, I also included a hard-coded convolution by an obscured Airy profile (following the example of the source I was looking at).

When trying to clean up the existing code, I thought I'd try out implementing the `VonKarman` profile by itself first, which I thought could be a useful `GSObject` on its own as a more realistic atmospheric profile than our existing `Kolmogorov`.

What I've discovered is that the Fourier-space profile of the von Karman object does not approach 0 as k -> infinity; it instead approaches a positive nonzero constant.  For some relatively innocent-looking combinations of (wavelength, r0, L0), in fact, this constant is greater than the default `gsparams->maxk_threshold` = 1e-3, which means I can't assign a finite maxk.  It also makes the surface brightness at the origin infinite (b/c the Hankel transform of a constant is a delta function), which complicates constructing a `OneDimensionalDeviate` for photon-shooting (and generally makes evaluating xValue difficult).  The problems get worse for the second-kick-without-airy profile as the asymptotic constant becomes even larger when you add the low-k cutoff.

I suppose assigning maxk = infinity may be okay, as whatever you end up convolving by will end up asserting its own maxk when drawing in fft mode.  I'm a bit more concerned about the radial profile for photon-shooting though.  My thought there is to decompose the Fourier profile into constant-piece + piece-that-tends-to-zero.  Shooting the appropriate fraction of delta function photons from constant-piece should be straightforward.  Shooting the remain photons seems like it should mirror what's done in other SBProfiles, with the one additional wrinkle that the piece-that-tends-to-zero's Hankel profile can dip negative before approaching zero.  (This feels a little weird to me, but may be okay?  Do any of our other radial profiles ever have negative values in their Hankel transforms?)

The other option is to just give up on an independent vonKarman and SecondKick altogether.  For the SecondKick in particular, the current working implementation that includes a hard-coded convolution by an Airy profile doesn't have the above problems with maxk and photon-shooting.  It also can't take advantage of some of the more advanced things in OpticalPSF though, like struts, since the Airy is permanently built in.

Thoughts?
Sure.  No rush.  Thanks.

Also, do note the warning @tagliala posted above: using pseudo elements with the SVG with JS method is a known trouble area and our current recommendation is simply to avoid it in that case and stick with the Webfonts with CSS method.
https://fontawesome.com/how-to-use/svg-with-js#pseudo-elements

However, what this issue highlights is that, in an environment like WordPress, you can't necessarily avoid the presence of pseudo elements in the DOM, because WordPress may have them. 

You _can_ however, avoid the use of pseudo elements in your own code if you want to use SVG, and then make sure to keep `searchPseudoElements: false`.

So the workarounds for this known issue seem to be:

1. Use pseudo elements in your code, but set `searchPseudoElements: false` when the WP Customizer is active
1. Don't use pseudo elements in your code, and leave `searchPseudoElements: false` all the time.

The initialize flag means: The cluster has been successfully initialized. (Perhaps renaming the key could be useful). The design being that a cluster which has been succesfully initialized should not be automatically reinitialized. 

What we are thinking of is the following:

1. Win race to initialize
2. Set initialize key with a small (minute?) ttl
3. Initialize pgdata
4. Verify correct initialization
5. Update initialize key, remote the ttl

This will ensure that only 1 member will be initializing at any point in time, but if it does not succeed, other members can still initialize.

Why are you installing the data to `pythondir`? If you used `$(prefix)/share`, GLib would pick up the schemas automatically (it checks `XDG_DATA_DIRS`).

For testing without installation, you can do something similar to what, for example, GNOME Music does:

1. Compile the schemas to the build dir: https://gitlab.gnome.org/GNOME/gnome-music/blob/44d21816a0b87f84f631faf82d3012a44355238c/meson_post_conf.py
2. Create a config template, that will extend the `XDG_DATA_DIRS` variable with the builddir: https://gitlab.gnome.org/GNOME/gnome-music/blob/44d21816a0b87f84f631faf82d3012a44355238c/gnome-music.in#L43
3. Configure the file: https://gitlab.gnome.org/GNOME/gnome-music/blob/44d21816a0b87f84f631faf82d3012a44355238c/meson.build#L73-89

The issue [happens on python2.7, regardless of the Django version](https://travis-ci.org/algolia/algoliasearch-django/builds/306709097):
![Test matrix results, Python2 failing](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1821404/33212980-b13f07f0-d125-11e7-9c9d-b59e42d50003.png)

This is due to [Python2's _defaulting to ASCII standard if no other encoding hint are given_](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0263/#defining-the-encoding). 

[Specifying the encoding](https://github.com/algolia/algoliasearch-django/commit/648be7bd4de1848f4df385eed6eedf1c3adf74ea) with `# coding=utf-8` is enough to [fix the error](https://travis-ci.org/algolia/algoliasearch-django/builds/306710500):
![Test matrix results, all passing](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1821404/33212997-cc777566-d125-11e7-8b6a-8f082a4bcea9.png)

For those who are facing the same issue; Workaround is to pass filters for `__key__` property at the time of query construction.
Example:
Instead of:
```python
query = datastore.Query('Kind')
query.add_filter('__key__', '>=', datastore.Key('Kind', '<startname>'))
query.add_filter('__key__', '<', datastore.Key('Kind', '<endname>'))
entities = query.fetch()
```
Use:
```python
start_key = datastore.Key('Kind', '<startname>')
end_key = datastore.Key('Kind', '<endname>')
queries = datastore.Query('Kind', filters=[('__key__', '>=', start_key), ('__key__', '<', end_key)])
entities = query.fetch()
```

I can confirm also encountering this error/limit with the same stack trace while trying to do a transcription. 
1. **OS type and version:** MacOS 10.13.6 (High Sierra)
2. **Python version and virtual environment information:** `Python 3.7.0` 
3. **google-cloud-python:** Core: 0.28.1, Speech: 0.35.0

Indirectly, this is caused by enabling diarization and how transcriptions are divided into several results which represent discrete chunks of the transcribed dialogue. With diarization enabled, the `words` array in each result's transcription `alternative` does not encapsulate just the words for that portion of the transcription but also includes all previous words recognized in the transcription up to that point. 

Or put more succinctly, if you have `N` words in a transcription, with diarization enabled, there are `N^2 / 2` words in the transcription result payload instead of just `N` (to simplify this to the worst case for bounding behavior).

I've found attempting to transcribe any audio file over ~20 minutes will result in this error (exact length varies due to the number of transcribed words returned).
I'll leave the issue open, as making those two functions public / better documented seems like a reasonable enhancement.

In Matlab, it's a struct array that stores cell arrays. The 'head' and 'desc' fields are cell arrays of strings, the 'entry' field is a cell array of cell arrays, and the 'len' field is a cell array of integers. As I understand, the Python equivalent is a dictionary containing lists of strings ('head' and 'desc'), lists of lists ('entry'), and lists of integers ('length').

With @oena, we just checked out R. My understanding is the following: we have a list of lists of lists. The outermost list is equal to the number of lines in the GMT. The next layer (i.e. each corresponding to a single line) is a named list where the names are 'head', 'desc', 'entry', and 'len' . Since you can't just store 'head' and 'desc' as strings, they're stored as single-element lists, and then 'entry' is a list with several elements. 'length' is a single-element list containing an integer.

The main difference is that in R, the outermost container is a list whose length is equal to the number of sets (similar to option 1 above). In Matlab, it's a dictionary storing lists, where each list's length equals the number of sets (no equivalent option proposed).

So... I dunno. Happy to discuss in person when you get back, Dave. @tnat1031 and @rajivnarayan, feel free to chime in.
Yep, this change definitely didn't work. I'd propose just deleting this branch. And definitely would be good to dig into this deeper.

R is a little unique because lists can also act as dictionaries in that
it's possible to access individual elements by ID or by index. So in that
sense the current GMT implementation can be used in similar ways as both
the current and proposed Python implementations.

Thinking more generally, while it would be nice to have these
implementations behave the same way in multiple languages, it might also be
worth thinking about whether each language-specific implementation takes
best advantage of that language's capabilities (or mitigates limitations).

The dictionary option (option 2) doesn't seem too much more complicated
than the list of dictionaries option, plus it also lets you access any
individual item by ID, which I think is a pretty common use case. So my
vote would be option 2.

Hey folks - just wanted to chime in here as well.  Like @markpeek says, I try to help out with the maintenance of troposphere - but things are crazy this time of year (I work at an education startup, and Back to School is our Black Friday - only it lasts for 8 weeks :)).  A few thoughts:

- totally down for extra maintainers - maybe looking at the top contributors for folks would be a good idea?  Heck, at this point @philtay has contributed more than me :) https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/graphs/contributors

- I think the hardest part of maintaining troposphere is the PRs.  A couple of things could help with this:
  - Get us to dynamically generating everything from the AWS Cloudformaton schema.  @markpeek has done a bunch of work torwards that in the past, but the schema is finicky and has (had?) some issues with naming collisions which were hard to resolve.  If someone had time to dive into this, that would help a ton.
  - More people in the community could dive into PR reviews - I think that'd help a lot.

- Once things are merged, it's usually pretty easy to make a release - but honestly, once things are merged you can use pip to pull the sha you want (or master, master tends to stay stable).  I've been doing this more and more in my own projects.

I'm totally down for a regular release schedule, and I'd be up for committing to that.  The downside of that is that it means that some features might miss "ship windows" because their PRs aren't reviewed/have issues that need to be resolved.  That said, I still believe that getting things reviewed is the hardest part here, not the actual release cycle since it's really easy to work around releases.

Actually I don’t it will be possible to add an assertion for the datatype constructor. 

`text_type` is simply an alias, not an actual type. It’s defined as:
```python
if PY2:
  text_type = unicode 
else:
  text_type = str
```

I think I'll need a little more information. Are you using the new services code for the spotify object? In general, add_to_queue does not accept just a uri, it needs a queueable item, which is an object that has a certain set of properties. You can see an example of this when using the functions for the local music library (soco.music_library.get_tracks) that it returns Didl objects which has all the right properties.

You should not need an external module in order to use Spotify, however, the music service code in SoCo is fairly new, so right now, a little work is required before the returned search results can be added to the queue. You can see an example of how to do it in this jist: https://gist.github.com/lawrenceakka/2d21dca590b4fa7e3af2

In the example, the ids are hardcoded, but they could be pulled out of the metadata returned by the search method on the service object.

Since this is fairly new code, I think it is expected to be a little hard to figure out, so no need to add extra info at this point.

I just did another test and realized `as_next` works only if shuffle is enabled.
It does not change the queue in any way. Rather, in shuffle mode the player selects the next title in beforehand, and `as_next=True` sets the new item as the next track.
Sonos uses this feature for the 'Play Next' command.
I have updated the docstring to point out that the argument only works in shuffle mode, and converted it to Napoleon syntax.
Please have a look at the changes and check whether, with this clarification, `as_next` also works for you.

@benjaoming 

> So I was thinking something like for the build command, then we don't get installer-specific stuff in the source dist:

The solution you described seems to take place in `./buildkite/build_apk.sh`. If that's the case, which I assume it is, since that's the only place the directories can be reliably assumed, we might into issues with passing in arguments that involve the counts of both the `kolibri-kivy` and `kolibri` repos.

You're right about `python-for-android` allowing for custom arguments in the build command --
 we're aren't using straight `python-for-android`, though. We use [`buildozer'](https://github.com/kivy/buildozer) to construct the command for us. 

We can override any value that `buildozer.spec` provides `python-for-android` by creating an environment variable, however `build_apk.sh` relies on a docker image to create the environment in which `kolibri-kivy` is set up to run. Here's the build command in [`Dockerfile`](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri-kivy/blob/master/Dockerfile):

```
# Build the APK
RUN buildozer android debug
```

 but I imagine the python instance being wrapped in Docker would complicate the passing down of  variables based off of 2 separate repos down to the environment it's being run?

>This should be opened as a Morango issue possibly?

Hmm, doesn't sound like it to me. The only thing Morango is doing is requiring the `future` module, which isn't problematic in itself (and will work fine for normal non-bundled distributions). It's the path modifications in Kolibri that are causing the broader issue, since we have a single `dist` folder that is created by installing modules using Python 2, which is then also used when running with Python 3. It's the same issue we had back in the day with `cherrypy` and other libs installing themselves differently depending on the Python version, which is why we were pointing our requirements to the git version rather than pypi for a while (until they had fixed this on their side).

In DB2, this is a valid query, if `MYDB2.Example` exists.
 
     SELECT {MYDB2.Example}.{id}, {MYDB2.Example}.{ARTNR} FROM {MYDB2.Example}

`{` and `}` are used for quoting in DB2 SQL.  Perhaps you haven't created the table or you don't have permissions?

This sequence of code works for me on my AS400 iSeries machine where `DOMEXQRY` is swapped `MYDB2`: 

    CREATE TABLE DOMEXQRY.Example (                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
        id int,                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                
        artnr varchar(255)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    )                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    SELECT {DOMEXQRY.Example}.{id}, {DOMEXQRY.Example}.{ARTNR} FROM {DOMEXQRY.Example}

Okay, I see the issue now. With `docker.containers.create` we attempted to flatten the arguments list for HostConfig directly into the call, but it can be a bit confusing when migrating over from 1.x. As a result, `ports` and `port_bindings` have been fused into one and you'll just need to provide the dictionary in the `create` call. Same idea for `volumes` and `binds`.

You'll want to try this instead:
```python
cont_create = docker.containers.create(image       = ship_opts['cont_image'],
                                              hostname    = ship_opts['cont_hostname'],
                                              ports       = {5520: 5520, 7088: 7088, 8088: 8088, 5020: 5020, 8181: 8181},
                                              command     = ship_opts['cont_action'],
                                              cpuset_cpus = ship_opts['cont_cpuset'],
                                              labels      = ship_opts['cont_label'],
                                              name        = ship_opts['cont_name'],
                                              environment = ship_opts['cont_env'],
                                              detach      = True,
                                               tty         = True,
                                              privileged     = True,
                                               dns            = ship_opts['cont_dns'],
                                    dns_search     = ship_opts['cont_search'],
                                    mem_limit      = ship_opts['cont_memory'][0],
                                    network_mode   = ship_opts['cont_network_type'],
                                    binds          = mount_points,
                                    restart_policy = {"MaximumRetryCount": 5, "Name": "always"}
)

After including the hacks from GlowingCrystalineEntity, I was still getting the same "file name too long" errors, but from a different location in the TraceBack.

For that reason, I added a (very dangerous) hack that might help some people above.  

In the file "downloaders.py", I add this snippet near LINE 370 (between for "progress.start()" and " f = open(filename, 'ab') if resume else open(filename, 'wb')"

"
            progress.start()

            ''' BEGINNING CUSTOM HACK  '''

            import os, statvfs
            maxFileNameLength = os.statvfs('/')[statvfs.F_NAMEMAX]

            lastChunk   = filename.split('/')[-1]
            if len(lastChunk) > maxFileNameLength:
                filename = filename.replace(lastChunk, lastChunk[:maxFileNameLength])
            ''' END CUSTOM HACK '''
            
            f = open(filename, 'ab') if resume else open(filename, 'wb')
``

The whole point is to identify the actual file being edited, assess the length of it's name. If it is larger than `maxFileNameLength` (defined individually by your OS), then just chop off the end and move on with your downloads.  On my end, I also added a bunch of print() statements to identify which file was a problem.  I plan to hunt down those files manually later.

Hi @rraadd88, I see you are using a reader that provides 3d frames. The thing with FramesSequence objects is, that they don't actually contain the image data (that would give memory issues), but only references where to read it on the hard drive.

So what you want is a so-called `pipeline`, which is rather new in pims and still undocumented. You'll need pims 0.3.3 and define a function:

    import pims
    @pims.pipeline
    def average_z(image):
        return image.sum(axis=0)  # the same as image[0] + ... + image[4]

This function is 'decorated' with a pims pipeline, which makes it recognize FramesSequence objects. If you then apply it to your `frames` object, it will create a new FramesSequence that holds reference to the older via the pipeline. Like this:

    new_frames = average_z(frames)
    new_frames[0]  # print the first frame of shape (512, 512)

If `start platform` hangs, it often indicates a fleet/etcd error internal to the cluster. As far as why vagrant didn't allocate our preferred IP address range, I've seen this happen in the case of collisions, where I already had a vagrant / VirtualBox VM using that address, when I've tried to use vagrant / VMware as well, or if VirtualBox's own network interfaces are somehow stale or incorrect. I can't say for sure that's what you're seeing, but my troubleshooting steps in this case are usually to 1) shut down all VirtualBox instances, and 2) remove the VirtualBox network bridges and dhcp servers:
```
$ VBoxManage list hostonlyifs
$ VBoxManage hostonlyif remove vboxnet1
$ VBoxManage hostonlyif remove vboxnet1
$ VBoxManage list dhcpservers
$ VBoxManage remove --netname HostInterfaceNetworking-vboxnet1
```
VirtualBox will recreate them the next time you start a VM, which--at least for me--usually fixes the assignment of 172.17.8.100.

Based on @tdelmas's clarification, I'm not sure what (for us) the best practices should be for the backup key. My interpretation of section 4.3 suggests that this key is something we'd have to deliver to the `deis` client at some point to be stored locally on the developer's workstation.

For convenience, here's the paragraph in question:

```
   The primary way to cope with the risk of inadvertent Pin Validation
   failure is to keep a Backup Pin.  A Backup Pin is a fingerprint for
   the public key of a secondary, not-yet-deployed key pair.  The
   operator keeps the backup key pair offline, and sets a pin for it in
   the PKP header.  Then, in case the operator loses control of their
   primary private key, they can deploy the backup key pair.  UAs, who
   have had the backup key pair pinned (when it was set in previous
   Valid Pinning Headers), can connect to the host without error.
```

(The reasoning for offline storage is explained in the following paragraph.)

I'd like to have a solid understanding of what the best practice for dealing with the backup key is. Is this something that the platform user(s) need to be aware of? Practically speaking, whose responsibility is it?

```

So I am thinking about this problem and since I'm not super familiar with how the internals work I can't say for sure if this is right.  But it seems like two things are needed:

1.  Ensuring it's a good regex during validation
2.  Compiling the string into a regex prior to use

On a whim I dove into the django source, guessing that the to_python function was getting called before, but not too far before the run_validators.  So I checked in on django/db/modeds/fields/__init__.py line 600:

```
    def clean(self, value, model_instance):
        """
        Convert the value's type and run validation. Validation errors
        from to_python and validate are propagated. The correct value is
        returned if no error is raised.
        """
        value = self.to_python(value)
        self.validate(value, model_instance)
        self.run_validators(value)
        return value
```

I got super lucky!  I found the exact right spot.  The self.to_python is where the RegexField is turning the string into an actual compiled regex and then returning it.

```
    def to_python(self, value):
        """
        Handles the following cases:
        1. If the value is already the proper type (a regex), return it.
        2. If the value is a string, compile and return the regex.

        Raises: A ValidationError if the regex cannot be compiled.
        """
        if isinstance(value, type(re.compile(''))):
            return value
        else:
            if value is None and self.null:
                return None
            else:
                try:
                    return self.get_compiled_regex(value)
                except:
                    raise ValidationError('Invalid regex {0}'.format(value))
```

I wonder if there isn't a way to make a custom validator that ensures the string is a legitimate regex (for case 1) during validation and then changing the value of the field during the post_init phase of the model lifecycle.  It seems like you'd also need a corresponding thing in the pre_save to turn the regex back into a pattern string before it gets saved also.

@bkreider I feel like there is a solution if there is no need to have authenticated access to some of the listed repository sources, e.g. anonymous access (or "failed auth token") to Anaconda Cloud.  My sense is that the logic flow should identify all repository sources and try ONCE for each with the set of auth tokens (which may be zero or one, or perhaps, if we support it, multiple tokens).  This will let the client know which tokens work with which repos, and only use the correct matching sets.  It may be that this process needs to be repeated at the per-channel level since perhaps different tokens are needed for different channels on the same repository.  But the flood of 401 errors that happen right now definitely seems to be unnecessary and avoidable.

> This is a different parser that keeps comments in yaml files. It is more robust than our hand-coded solution (which is removed by this commit). The library is ruamel.yaml (https://pypi.python.org/pypi/ruamel.yaml, fork of pyyaml), which has also replaced pyyaml in the cookiecutter project (audreyr/cookiecutter#558)
>

@kalefranz third commit: the Appveyor failures are benign. They are caused because `TemporaryDirectory()` expects to be able to clean itself up without errors.

I fixed this in the third commit by replacing them with `make_temp_env()` calls. I had already changed this function to skip any conda calls if there are no arguments---in which case it basically functions the same as `TemporaryDirectory()` but ignoring any errors on cleanup.

I think the symlinks still have a big worth, because they allow to save a lot of space. This may not be important on the ever growing local disks, but on a shared filesystem the home directories of users often need to be limited. The symlinks allow for minimal environments where most of the links will point to the centrally installed packages, some links point to the users own package cache with newly downloaded packages.

Sure, the drawback is tha these environments are not truly well isolated, and messing with the package cache could make some of them unusable. But I still think that the symlinks have their value.

The situation can be repaired if one makes a simple exception for the installation of python itself. If I e.g. just copy the pkg-cache python to the symlinks location:
```
(pytest)$ ls -l pytest/bin/python2.7 
lrwxr-xr-x 1 feichtinger unx-ait 78 Jul  2 09:12 pytest/bin/python2.7 -> ~/.conda/envs/.pkgs/python-2.7.12-0/bin/python2.7

(pytest)$ rm pytest/bin/python2.7

(pytest)$ cp ~/.conda/envs/.pkgs/python-2.7.12-0/bin/python2.7 pytest/bin/python2.7
```
Now the environment works fine for me.

For the moment I will recommend our users to do this copying manually as a fast workaround. But I think it would be great if conda would implement this exception for the python binary, because it would make the symlink option work nicely.

Best regards,
Derek

Actually, it doesn't care.  The pattern is just `^.*(timestamp pattern).*$`.  And because it's just matching digits: 4 numbers, a period, 2 numbers, a period, and 2 numbers; Then weeks match the pattern, but it can't be reverse translated to `%d` for days.  That's what the error is actually doing.   Yes, `prefix` is not being filtered first, but that's why it sees the pattern there.

So, to your point, if you were to have `monthly-%Y.%m` and `daily-%Y.%m.%d`, without applying prefix first, the above timestamp pattern would match because 4 digits followed by a period, followed by 2 digits still matches the `daily` index type too.  Curator 4 allows you to get around this by using an extra filter with exclude set to True, e.g.

```
- filtertype: pattern
  kind: timestring
  value: '%Y.%m'
  exclude: False
- filtertype: pattern
  kind: timestring
  value: '%Y.%m.%d'
  exclude: True
```
Without calculating any dates, this would filter to keep only 4 digit + `.` + 2 digit timestring patterns.

Of course, it could also be handled by prefix first, too, should the prefixes differ.

I'm kind of confused that you are trying to connect to localhost in your configuration file, but are using AWS IAM credentials.  I will address each of the scenarios I can conceive of, based on what I'm seeing.

## IAM Credentials and AWS ES
The first thing you should know, before you go any farther, is that Curator 4 does not work with AWS ES and IAM credentials: https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/faq_aws_iam.html

They remain as place-holders in case Amazon AWS ES ever allows the cluster state metadata endpoint in the future.


The main reason for your observation is at https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v4.1.2/curator/utils.py#L558

The override only happens if the AWS IAM key, secret key and region (all three) are set. If you're connecting with IAM credentials, it forces use_ssl to True, because that's how those work, and that is required.  That's why it is forcing SSL, even though you haven't set that.

## Elasticsearch on own EC2 instances
If you are using your own Elasticsearch nodes in your own AWS EC2 instances, then you do not need IAM credentials, and they won't work for you in this case anyway.  Don't populate those fields in the configuration YAML file if this is the case.

> What is the issue with new verb?

It is against the CLI convention that commands which `new up a thing` should be named as `create`; otherwise, people have no clear clue to differentiate between the existing `create` and the new command called `new`.
Once put it in the extension, I would suggest name like `quick-create`, `create-nodejs-web`, etc.

Hi @voiddragon. VM create is intended for creating VMs that are essentially blank except for the OS image. If you run `az vm image list` you will get the URNs for some common OS images that should all work with `az vm create`. There are also aliases for some of these, called out in `az vm create -h`. You can also provide a URI to an OS disk image VHD.

If you run `az vm list --all` you will get a list of all images from the Marketplace. Some of these will work, but many of them will not because they go beyond simply configuring a VM with an OS by having pre-installed software or configurations that require additional Azure resources beyond what `az vm create` is allowed to provision. In your case, because it is using WordPress, it requires an app service plan. 

If you would like to create a Marketplace offering like the Bitnami image with WordPress that you tried, you should create that through the portal as it has a different engine that allows it prompt for those things. In the meantime I will keep this issue open until we decide how to address that `az vm image list --all` returns URNs which are not supported.

There's a couple questions here so I'll address them separately :D

### detecting deleted files

pre-commit currently excludes `deleted` files from the list of files it passes to hooks:

https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/823c5bc8c3a9f713ce22849792be50d4a2a23f42/pre_commit/git.py#L84-L85

That said, you can still get the behavior you want through a couple of ways:

From [the docs](https://pre-commit.com/#new-hooks) some combination of `always_run: true` and `pass_filenames: false` enables a tool to be executed disregarding the staged files.  From that you could call `git diff --staged --diff-filter=D` to get deleted files directly (I forget whether this follows moves or not, you'll want to double check that!).  Note that there's a couple hooks in `pre-commit-hooks` that do [something similar](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/904a06196df83e5ba5563d0a839103ade2de16b6/pre_commit_hooks/util.py#L12-L15) (but for newly added only).

### returns zero but prints

There's a newish option for hooks, `verbose: true` -- while it's an option, I personally discourage it as it leads to an increase in "warning noise".

### separate lists for added / modified / deleted

Currently no, I also don't really want to add such a feature, if a hook wants this information it can already compute it (and there's precedence for this approach).


Hope this helps!

 use strip() , strip('\n'), rstrip('\n'),   it is OK , because nothing to do.

Are you launching Jupyter Notebook from a terminal, or from a GUI launcher of some kind?

from https://github.com/codemirror/CodeMirror/issues/150

If you would like to increase/decrease the font size in the cells, then run the following code instead:

```py
%%html
<style type='text/css'>
.CodeMirror{
    font-size: 17px;
</style>
```

Oh that's a shame :(. Even `dot` sucks in some cases because BLAS doesn't (yet) support arbitrarily strided arrays. There was a good description of how numpy deals with different strides posted [here](http://numpy-discussion.10968.n7.nabble.com/RFC-comments-to-BLAS-committee-from-numpy-scipy-devs-td45092.html):

>how do you perform a GEMM operation on two arrays with arbitrary strides? Currently, NumPy attempts to detect a number of special cases: if the strides in both arrays imply a column-major layout, then call BLAS directly; if one of them has strides corresponding to a row-major layout, then set the corresponding `transA`/`transB` argument, etc. – and if all else fails, either copy the data into a contiguous buffer, or else fall back on a naive triple-nested-loop GEMM implementation. (There's also a check where if we can determine through examining the arrays' data pointers and strides that they're actually transposes of each other, then we instead dispatch to SYRK.)

I've tried that combination recently to [use some scipy.special functions from numba](http://pybonacci.org/2016/02/07/como-crear-extensiones-en-c-para-python-usando-cffi-y-numba/) and it works like a charm (in Spanish, sorry :relaxed:)

It's tantalising for derivatives of sin and cos too because calculating `1 - x**2` is way quicker than calculating sin/cos (around 8x faster on my laptop), but you need to also calculate the correct sign, which slows things back down, at least in my implementation attempts.

@olson-sean-k and I talked about this today, and we decided not to support it. Our biggest worry is that by supporting it, we'd put a burden on any tool that wants to be peru-aware to know all the different filenames peru might be using.

Agreed that mixing extensions sucks. (We have some of the same with `.travis.yml`.) We went with `.yaml` because that seems to be the Official Extension [suggested by the YAML designers](http://www.yaml.org/faq.html).

Looks like we could do a few things (maybe not all of these are worth the effort):

- Cache the `peru.yaml` file that we read from module trees. This one's a must.
- Have `Cache.export_tree` short-circuit after checking status when the imports haven't changed.
  - We could save the index file that was created when lastimports was written. (We could put the tree hash in the file name to make sure we don't use something that's out of date.)
  - We might need `update-index` to recreate missing files, but we could do a preliminary `diff-index` to see if there even are any missing files. The fast path would be when there aren't, and the slow path would rerun `diff-index` to make sure the working copy isn't actually dirty.

In a perfect world, the no-op sync would be a single `diff-index` call.

This is the same error discussed above with @esstokes1 and @sbingner. Your client certificate **must** have the extended key usage extension with client authentication set to be used for client authentication with PyKMIP.

According to the KMIP specification:

> Conformant KMIP servers SHALL use the identity derived from the channel mutual authentication to determine the client identity...

Since KMIP servers/clients must use TLS to conform with the KMIP specification, the identity must be derived from the client's certificate.

For more information on client authentication with certificates, see RFC 5280. If you are creating your certificates yourself, you will need to use OpenSSL (or something equivalent) to include that extension during the certificate creation process. If you are obtaining your certificates from a third-party, you will need to have them add the extension for you.

Whoa, quite a discussion here, folks!

We're using Flake8 extensively where I work so I have some experience with this:
- changing the entry point *seems* to work because both entry points get registered; but then your user configs can't for example ignore Bugbear's B3xx warnings while keeping Bandit's B3xx warnings. As far as Flake8 is concerned, both are the same warning now.
- changing codes to use more than one character might not work with your editor integrations (like Syntastic) because the authors often use a very limited regex that was only tested with the builtin warnigns. It would be helpful if Flake8 itself shipped with multi-letter warnings, this way educating integrators that matching a single uppercase letter and three digits is not enough.

Given the two problems I outlined above, I see two possibilities:
1. either flake8-bandit renames bandit Bxxx codes into Sxxx; or
2. rename bugbear codes into BBxxx; or
3. merge the two plugins? Security problems is an interesting class that I think could fit in Bugbear, too.

If we choose the first solution, that solves the error code clash forever.

If we choose to rename Bugbear codes, the .flake8 configuration of existing projects in the wild will become invalid. Plus, I will have to deal with non-working integrations like outlined above.

If we choose to merge, renaming B30x in Bugbear to B350 is only going to help in the short term because as soon as Bandit reaches B351 then we're conflicting again.

In general, the age of Bandit itself doesn't matter in this issue since this is a separate tool unrelated to Flake8 and can use whatever output it desires. It doesn't have to compose.

I think 1. is easiest, but 3. is probably wisest long-term. What do you think?

This appears to be a `boulder` problem. The "Self-verify" message is the client trying to validate the challenge itself before it notifies `boulder` that the challenge has been set up.

The restriction on `Content-Type` has recently been removed from the ACME spec. The self-validation code currently still requires this, but we only show a warning that "Self-verify of challenge failed" but continue to notify `boulder` that the challenge has been set up.

After fixing the `Content-Type`, the client was able to successfully validate the challenge, but `boulder` still failed to connect. I've notified the `boulder` team of this issue and hopefully we can get back to you shortly.

Per-message errors are only provided through the delivery report callback (on_delivery), the error_cb is for more generic/global errors.

If you are only interested in delivery failures you can provide a global `on_delivery` callback in the Producer() config dict and also set `delivery.report.only.error`.
E.g.:
```
p = confluent_kafka.Producer({"bootstrap.servers": "thelot", "on_delivery": my_dr_cb, "delivery.report.only.error": True})
```

> I'm going to put a vote in favor of excluding dump-only fields when they're passed in. The dump-only field is "known," it's just a one-way sort of deal.

I think describes it well.

I can try to make PR for this. I just need to understand it better first because:

Similar problem seems to exist with ``unknown=RAISE``: ``dump_only`` fields are first skipped in deserialization, but then regarded as unknown, which will lead to error if you you have ``unknown=RAISE``. Wouldn't it be better to ignore the dump-only fields during loading and not raise error on them (as we're explicitly saying we are not interested about these fields during loading)?

It seems that current logic was explicitly added in https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/pull/865 but I'm wondering if that's the right call? While it's consistent with current state of ``INCLUDE``, it's against the intended behavior that this ticket suggests, which would be to:

1) Exclude ``dump_only`` fields during ``load()`` with ``unknown=INCLUDE`` (they are now included), and I think we should also similarly:
2) Not validate ``dump_only`` fields during ``load()`` with ``unknown=RAISE`` (they are now validated)

The idea being that in both cases, like @rileyjohngibbs said, ``dump_only`` fields are not really _unknown_ but rather known and ignored.

> Of course, it would be really nice to have a way to configure this, so we can each have our own opinions, but maybe that's ugly.

Adding more ``unknown`` options like ``INCLUDE_ONLY`` or ``EXCLUDE_ALL`` wouldn't necessarily turn ugly.

For now should be able to work around the issue by renaming input and output features of your model which have a '-' in them. 

e.g. try doing something like this:
```
import coremltools
model_spec = coremltools.utils.load_spec('YourModel.mlmodel')
coremltools.utils.rename_feature(model_spec, 'conv4-2', 'conv4_2')
coremltools.utils.save_spec(model_spec,'YourModelRenamed.mlmodel')
```

Note that internal layer inputs and output names with '-' should be fine. The bug only impacts names that are exposed as input or output features of the model.

OK, this is pretty tricky. But we use this for such an use case:

```python
# We need a function which is also a string. Function is used in dehydrating
# ManyToManyField, string is used in apply_sorting to create an order_by argument.
class RelatedNodes(str):
    def __call__(self, bundle):
        # Add name to related nodes
        return bundle.obj.related_nodes.regpoint('config').registry_fields(
            name='core.general#name',
        )
```

And then in the resource itself, we can define such field:

```python
    related_nodes = api_fields.ManyToManyField(to=NodeResource, attribute=RelatedNodes('related_nodes__config_core_generalconfig__name'), full=True)
```

So the code in `RelatedNodes` is our dynamic schema ORM based on Django. So in this way we can by setting `attribute` allow a constant string and also a dynamic string.

It's too late to rename because people might rely on it, we could just add an alias. I usually use fully-qualified names for both, or do something like `floatX = theano.config.floatX` if I need it a lot in a particular function.

> I think that instead of documenting for leaky_rectify and very_leaky_rectify you should just document it for any slope alpha as in the PReLU paper.

Yes, that's what I suggested: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/issues/468#issuecomment-149212511

> You can also and just add another `init` class which takes as argument the slope of the relu and calls the correct initializer with the correct gain.

That's complicated, there are several initializers (six in total) with a `gain` argument, so we'd either need six subclasses or a single class that takes an initializer class as an argument, which would be weird to use. Instead, we could just add a helper function that gives the gain factor for a nonlinearity, as I suggested: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/issues/468#issuecomment-149190199
But documenting it is a good first step.

> it's odd that this only comes up now

No, if you set `p` to a Python float instead of a numpy scalar, then everything's fine. We should allow both, though.

> I think a more future-proof fix is probably retain_prob = np.array(1 - self.p, dtype=theano.config.floatX).

Nope, `p` could be symbolic. It should be `retain_prob = lasagne.utils.floatX(1) - self.p`.

`dataset.cam0` is a property that creates a new generator each time you access it, so calling `next(dataset.cam0)` will only ever return the first frame. This is just syntactic sugar to avoid explicitly rewinding the dataset every time you want to iterate through it. The usual thing you'll need to do is something like this:
```python
for image in dataset.cam0:
     do_something(image)
```
which works as you'd expect.

If you need to access arbitrary frames, you have a few options:
1. Pass a `frames` argument to the constructor to load only specific frames like I do [here](https://github.com/utiasSTARS/pykitti/blob/master/demos/demo_raw.py#L23). You can pass an arbitrary list instead of a range if need be.
2. Use the `itertools.islice` method like I do [here](https://github.com/utiasSTARS/pykitti/blob/master/demos/demo_raw.py#L34).
3. Create a list from the generator (this can use up a lot of memory): 
```python
cam0_frames = list(dataset.cam0)
image01 = cam0_frames[1]
```
4. Create another variable to point to a specific instance of the generator, and use `next` like you were trying to do: 
```python
cam0_generator = dataset.cam0
image00 = next(cam0_generator)
image01 = next(cam0_generator)
```

Say you set the `app` with `extra_environ`:

```
        self.app = DjangoTestApp(
            extra_environ={'HTTP_HOST': self.tenant.domain_url}
        )
```

When making a request (`self.app.get(...)`) the host is reset to `testserver`.
So you have to set it on every request:
```

        response = self.app.get(
            reverse('entries:create'),
            user=self.user1,
            extra_environ={'HTTP_HOST': self.tenant.domain_url}
        )
```

or create a new app class and override each HTTP method, method to use the environ set on the clients `extra_environ`

I need to add a proper nested XML object test now to make sure that this is unchanged as well as potentially a more modern take on the Schema test which just in isolation tests the creation, flush and load of all objects in the GPI.

1)
The removal of the internal lock is due to the fact that getting the read lock on an object leaves you vulnerable to the object changing underneath you or worse when the getter is responsible for 'construction on the fly' of objects internal to the GangaObject.
Getting the const lock externally is the only thing which I'd argue really makes sense as it prevents the object being changed while you're doing something to it which could change it.


2)
The main thing I'm trying to tackle in the getter is:
 
1) During the construction of an object the `_data` dict isn't populated by default (in this PR).
  This means performing `__init__` on an object is now much, _much_ faster.
 2) We then attempt to get some object from `_data`.
 3a) If the object is loaded then we need to generate it based on the default Schema value
 3b) If the object is __not__ loaded then we need to load it from disk and return the value.

In solving 3) if we don't know the current loaded state we can't correctly work out whether we should return the default value from the schema or the value from disk.

The reason there is a check around the `getReadLock` is due to a problem that calling this multiple times on a loaded job causes issues (strange lockups due to some of the Registry code triggering the full `_data` dictionary to become populated probably due to some flush to disk type event).
I _only_ want to load an object into memory here so anything that the Registry does aside from this is not expected.
This is a problem lower down than in the GangaObject but can be avoided by not making multiple calls to this method.
I could fix this but it would likely involve some invasive changes I was hoping to avoid for data integrity sake.
As this method is used trigger a load from disk you in theory should never need to call it multiple times. We could and probably should be calling `self._getRegistry()._load(someID)` here anyway to load the object which is _much_ clearer.


If it wasn't for the very large performance improvements this gets I wouldn't be pushing for it, there is a very large up-front cost to constructing things like a 100k+ DiracFile objects, and whilst we can prevent some situations where this may happen we won't likely get all of them.

3) Which functions are having the synchronised statements removed? This is likely a typo. I'm adding it in several places but don't remember removing any.

Your questions confused me initially, but I think that you are asking why support for Graph API version 2.6 is not available when you install facebook-sdk.

If this is the case, it is because the most recent version (1.0.0) was released **before** Graph API 2.6 was released. To use any of Graph API 2.6's features, you will want to [install the SDK from Git](https://facebook-sdk.readthedocs.io/en/latest/install.html#installing-from-git).

Questions like this are better suited to [our Google Group](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/pythonforfacebook), which you should join! I will post a more detailed explanation of the different versions (and why you might want to install them) there soon.

Hi -- the Creative Labs VF0800 "Senz3D" camera is not supported. I do believe it is actually a USB 2.0 device, but it is not a UVC compliant camera (you would need to poke some on-device registers and hack its isochronous streaming protocol). To recap what we talked about on Bluejeans:

* flake8 calls pycodestyle.
* flake8 and pylint both check for certain things, like line length. It'd be good if just one linter checked for those things. This can be accomplished by adding `F401,E501` to the make file.
* The `noqa` pragmas ignore too much stuff.
* This will drop all plain `noqa` pragmas from the code base: `git grep --files-with-matches noqa . | xargs sed -i 's/noqa //'`.

> There are plenty of other modules that can benefit from this.

Another option is to use a lazy-loader for SQLAlchemy that is more intelligent about bulk loading. We wrote a custom loader that will inspect the DB session and bulk-load all relations on similar models whenever a relation is lazy-loaded. The result should be the equivalent to using `subqueryload` on all relations upfront, but without needing any custom code to do so. The loader we wrote is available here in case it's helpful to anyone else: https://github.com/operator/sqlalchemy_bulk_lazy_loader

```
debug_panel.middleware.DebugPanelMiddleware

I think it's conceptually cleaner to keep everything in `mTlvs` and not special case the timestamp as is currently done.  Part of the reason why I did special case `mTimestamp` is because all of the comparisons are done using the timestamp.  I see three options every time you need to reference the timestamp:

1. Search for the timestamp every time.
2. Maintain a pointer to the timestamp.
3. Keep the timestamp in a well-known location (this is what's implemented today).

I'm fine with moving to either of the other alternatives if you find the logic to be simpler.

Content of `foliant.yml`:

```yaml
preprocessors:
    - includes:
        aliases:
            cliar_none: https://github.com/moigagoo/cliar.git
            cliar_develop: https://github.com/moigagoo/cliar.git#develop
```

Content of `index.md`:

```xml
<include>$cliar_none#develop$README.md</include>
<include>$cliar_develop$README.md</include>
<include>$cliar_develop#master$README.md</include>
<include>$https://github.com/moigagoo/cliar.git#develop$README.md</include>
<include>$https://github.com/moigagoo/cliar.git$README.md</include>
```

What we get:

* `repo_url`: always `https://github.com/moigagoo/cliar.git`;
* `revision`:
    * `develop`;
    * `develop`;
    * `master` (revision specified in the include statement overrides the revision specified in config);
    * `develop`;
    * `master`.

It's more common to see this sort of thing on non-optimizing debug builds.  Compilers don't *have* to replace `constexpr` values with the literal value, but it's an easy optimization to make, so most do.  But under `-O0` (i.e. no optimization), the compiler sometimes references rather than substituting the `constexpr` value.

My gut feeling is that explicitly specifying return value policies make sense for return values, but explicitly specifying input argument policies seems like it'd introduce an extra layer of complication (versus simply treating mutable lvalue references as reference-only, no copy).

Can I ask what workflows there are where a (temporary) copy of an object for a mutable lvalue reference is actually desirable?

That being said, it may line up more nicely with ownership transfer, like @torokati44's PR #1226.
Though for that, if this were to be used for ownership), what would the policy be for `take_ownership` (implying a contextual definition)? Or would it be a different flag, that's not really a return-value policy per-se, but a casting policy?
And what would the interplay look like for holder types? (Transferring ownership to C++ for a copyable holder doesn't make any sense, and you can't `release()` a `shared_ptr` if the input argument is a pointer.)

(I do have a little bit of bias here, as it'd be nice to let referencing / ownership be implicit for arguments, as they are done in C++, through the use of mutable lvalue references, `unique_ptr`, etc :) )

Regarding the question of actions that cause costs for users: you have the option of calling `IDisplay` methods (the Apache plugin does [quite a bit of that](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot-apache/certbot_apache/display_ops.py)) to get permission from the user before incurring costs.

There's currently no across-the-board method for triggering `enhance` calls from the client; we're probably going to add some infrastructure of that sort as we work on #1123, though I'm not sure it makes sense to pick up arbitrary enhancements from plugins and offer them to the user directly via UI.

Ah good point. When we start overriding parser functionality, were you planning on putting the overridden methods in the same Override class or in a new one for the parser? While it probably doesn't matter too much, I think it's probably best to put it in a separate class as it breaks things up into smaller pieces that are easier to digest and tries to help keep the functionality provided by the configurator and parser distinct.

If we go this route, we could do something like defining a `create_apache_parser` method in the base configurator that returns a new Apache parser. The base configurator could use this method which could be overridden by any distro specific derived classes to use a different parser object.

Thoughts? We definitely don't have to go with this approach and I'm not necessarily opposed to going with what you've done so far. My main concern with the current approach is it is custom code to keep track of the base configurator object and method which gets handled automatically by Python when overriding class methods using inheritance.

How about we define some `bucket` for task queues? Something like:

1. PBS defines bucket size (cores, mem, walltime etc).
2. When PBS tries to submit jobs, it tries to fit multiple jobs into buckets according to task properties.
3. Multiple jobs are executed either in parallel or sequentially depending on the shape of bucket.

A bucket is actually a special task that execute other tasks. It can be made transparent in the sense that users only see individual tasks and their properties.

One of the biggest problems here is that after the execution of each substep, sos needs to `glob` all `.out` files, which increases linearly with increasing `_index`. So while my test here with `5000` files completes reasonably fast, `50000` can be hundreds of times slower.

PyTorch wants RNN inputs in that form because cuDNN does, and cuDNN does because batch-first would mean that the tensor that contains the input for one timestep wouldn’t be contiguous.
If you’re using a 1D convolution or another kind of layer, you may have to transpose first (but it’s unlikely to be a major performance bottleneck in your code).

I'm not sure I understand this question or response.  Do we want Pulumi Python users to be able to use `async/await` and other Python 3 features where they feel like it?  Of course.  Do we want the API design of the Pulumi SDK to encourage/require use of `async/await` or other features? More important is a simple an idiomatic API design that works nicely for both Python 2 and Python 3 users.

Hi there,

Thanks for the interest and the PR, it's a good idea to include per-host proxy configuration as well. However, I do not think the current implementation is appropriate for this project.

The PR as is would create a host config dict for each and every host, whether per-host configuration was supplied for that host or not.

Firstly, a dict per host with a large number of hosts would mean large amounts of memory being used to store all those dicts. Python dicts use a lot of memory even if empty (see ```sys.getsizeof({})```). Not to mention that the per host dictionary is re-generated on every call to `run_command` and how long that would take with a large host list.

Host numbers in the multiple thousands or even hundred or more thousands are to be expected in this project, see design and goals. Therefore it would be best to use a host config dict only when host configuration is supplied and not to generate new ones after object is instantiated.

Secondly, the use of ParallelSSHClient object attributes inside the dictionary smells. What if those object attributes are changed/added to?

Is having to change object attributes on the class constructor as well as an internal function with static attribute names appropriate?

Additional comments inline.

Changes are also lacking tests :)

The only issue I have with that explanation is that timeout should never get hit. The command itself takes 2 sec to execute and even if I put the timeout to 60s, the output gets truncated. Is that also expected behavior, if so, sounds very counter intuitive.

model top pressure is decided a priori, and `air_pressure_on_interface_levels` must describe a pressure

Here is some py.test code that I use to catch never awaited warnings. First it test that the warning actually occur, and then it tests my 'in_parallel_do' function. The basic idea is `with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as w` in setup, and `gc.collect()` + ` assert w == []`in the test harness teardown phase. 
def test_unawaited_tasks():
    import gc
    import pytest
    import warnings

    async def nop(name):
        return name

    gc.collect()
    with pytest.warns(RuntimeWarning):
        nop(3)
        gc.collect()   # To raise RuntimeWarning if we have un-awaited tasks.

    class MyException(Exception):
        pass
    async def raiser():
        raise MyException

    with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as w:
        with pytest.raises(MyException):
            in_parallel_do(nop(1), raiser(), nop(3))
        gc.collect()   # To raise RuntimeWarning if we have un-awaited tasks.

    assert w == []

@whatdhack, @ahundt, Please note that:  

|                                       | bottleneck             |
|---------------------------------------|------------------------|
| ResNet50                              | avg_pool (1x1)         |
| VGG16, VGG19                          | block5_pool (7x7)      |
| DenseNet121, DenseNet169, DenseNet201 | bn (7x7)               |
| InceptionV3                           | mixed10 (8x8)          |
| InceptionResNetV2                     | conv_7b_ac (8x8)       |
| MobileNet                             | conv_pw_13_relu (7x7)  |
| MobileNetV2                           | out_relu (7x7)         |
| NASNetLarge                           | activation_260 (11x11) |
| NASNetMobile                          | activation_188 (7x7)   |

All except `ResNet50` (not `VGG`) have the same size bottlenecks (just before the statement `if include_top`). In most models' blocks, 2x-downsampling is followed by convs, but in `VGG`s' blocks, the downsampling follows convs. Thus, it is not so good to decouple `block5_pool` from `block5_conv1`, `block5_conv2`, and `block5_conv3`, because they are originally proposed as a single unit block.

When `include_top=False`, I think it is good to return a 7x7 feature map for a consistency over `keras-applications`. However, it is reasonable to return a 14x14 feature map if there are many use-cases. The Faster RCNN uses `block5_conv3` (14x14) instead of `block5_pool` (7x7). @whatdhack, Is there any references using a 14x14 feature map as a final feature, besides the Faster RCNN?

If there are not many examples, using the method like `image_model_output = model.layers[-2]` proposed by @ahundt is better than breaking the consistency.
